title: Listing Platform — System Design
description: Design a large-scale listing platform that ingests listings from external systems (FTP + API) and manual + AI-assisted authoring, runs them through validation, normalization, enrichment, deduplication, fraud scoring, and media processing, then publishes the survivors into a near-real-time search index for seekers on web and mobile. The core tension is operating a high-throughput, multi-tenant write pipeline behind a low-latency, always-available public read surface — while keeping lifecycle (create/update/delete/expire), search relevance, media safety, product features (saved searches, alerts, abuse reports, owner analytics), and trust-and-safety (audit, GDPR, explainable moderation) all correct.

nodes.label: Listing Owner
nodes.description: Listing Owner: Company, agency, or individual who publishes listings.

nodes.label: External System
nodes.description: External System: Owner's ATS / CRM / DMS / inventory system pushing listings.

nodes.label: Seeker
nodes.description: Seeker: Public user searching for listings on web or mobile.

nodes.label: Platform Admin
nodes.description: Platform Admin: Internal operator running moderation, taxonomy, and policies.

nodes.label: Seeker Web / Mobile App
nodes.description: Seeker Web / Mobile App: Public seeker surface: website, mobile app, SEO landing pages.

nodes.label: Admin Panel
nodes.description: Admin Panel: Owner + platform-admin web app for listings, jobs, moderation, RBAC.

nodes.label: CDN / Edge Cache
nodes.description: CDN / Edge Cache: Caches seeker pages, listing detail JSON, and static media.

nodes.label: Public Search API
nodes.description: Public Search API: Read API for seeker search/detail/saved-search, fronted by CDN.

nodes.label: Owner / Partner API
nodes.description: Owner / Partner API: Authenticated write API for external systems: create/update/bulk/status/delete.

nodes.label: Seeker BFF
nodes.description: Seeker BFF: Backend-for-frontend that composes search results, listing detail, and personalization.

nodes.label: SFTP Endpoint
nodes.description: SFTP Endpoint: Per-owner SFTP directories; landing zone for CSV/XML/JSON (incl. ZIP/GZIP) uploads.

nodes.label: Raw File Store
nodes.description: Raw File Store: Object storage holding raw uploaded files for audit, replay, and reprocessing.

nodes.label: File Watcher / Scheduler
nodes.description: File Watcher / Scheduler: Detects new SFTP files, registers ImportJob rows, emits import events; detects duplicate files via content hash.

nodes.label: Stream Parser
nodes.description: Stream Parser: Streams CSV/XML/JSON row-by-row from object storage so 10M-row files don't OOM.

nodes.label: Schema Validator
nodes.description: Schema Validator: Per-row schema + business-rule validation; emits per-row pass/fail.

nodes.label: Listing Event Bus
nodes.description: Listing Event Bus: Kafka-style log partitioned by owner_id + listing_id; carries every lifecycle event (versioned).

nodes.label: Outbox / CDC
nodes.description: Outbox / CDC: Transactional outbox tailing the listing DB so downstreams never miss a state change.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Holds poison rows/messages for inspection and replay; per-stage routing.

nodes.label: Listing Store
nodes.description: Listing Store: Source of truth: listings (versioned), owners, lifecycle state, contact (encrypted), audit links.

nodes.label: Import & Audit Store
nodes.description: Import & Audit Store: ImportJob / ImportRecord / AuditLog / WebhookDelivery / AuthoringEvent rows.

nodes.label: Media / Image Store
nodes.description: Media / Image Store: Immutable original + derived (thumbnails, transcodes) media for listings; CDN-fronted.

nodes.label: Normalization Worker
nodes.description: Normalization Worker: Canonicalizes fields (location, price, currency, units) and maps owner taxonomy to ours.

nodes.label: Enrichment Orchestrator
nodes.description: Enrichment Orchestrator: Drives the enrichment plan: classification, geocoding, language, quality scores.

nodes.label: ML Model Service
nodes.description: ML Model Service: Serves classification, embedding, translation, and image-analysis models.

nodes.label: Geocoding Service
nodes.description: Geocoding Service: Address -> normalized location + lat/lng; calls external provider with caching.

nodes.label: Duplicate Detection
nodes.description: Duplicate Detection: Exact + fuzzy dedup using external_id, content hash, and embedding similarity.

nodes.label: External-ID Map
nodes.description: External-ID Map: (owner_id, external_listing_id) -> listing_id index for deterministic dedup.

nodes.label: Content Hash & Embedding Index
nodes.description: Content Hash & Embedding Index: MinHash/SimHash + vector index for near-duplicate lookups (blocking + ANN).

nodes.label: Fraud Scoring Service
nodes.description: Fraud Scoring Service: Combines rules + ML signals into a fraud risk score; logs decisions.

nodes.label: Feature Store
nodes.description: Feature Store: Owner reputation, behavior counters, suspicious-IP features for fraud scoring.

nodes.label: Rules Engine
nodes.description: Rules Engine: Hot-deployable rule packs (banned phrases, regex, owner suspensions).

nodes.label: Moderator Review Queue
nodes.description: Moderator Review Queue: Holds-for-review queue for fraud-flagged, suspected-duplicate, and seeker-reported listings.

nodes.label: AI Assistance Service
nodes.description: AI Assistance Service: LLM-backed: title/desc generation, category suggestion, policy detection, translation — PII-redacted; suggestions never auto-publish.

nodes.label: Media Processing Worker
nodes.description: Media Processing Worker: Fetches owner-provided media URLs, transcodes, generates thumbnails, writes immutable media objects.

nodes.label: Media Safety Scanner
nodes.description: Media Safety Scanner: Virus scan, EXIF/PII strip, image-moderation classifiers (NSFW/unsafe), copyright reuse fingerprint.

nodes.label: Saved-Search Alert Worker
nodes.description: Saved-Search Alert Worker: Consumes new/updated listings and matches them against saved seeker searches; enqueues notifications.

nodes.label: Rate-Limit / Quota Store
nodes.description: Rate-Limit / Quota Store: Per-owner token-bucket counters and import-concurrency leases; fast atomic increment+expire.

nodes.label: Search Indexer
nodes.description: Search Indexer: Consumes outbox/event bus, projects listings into SearchDocs, handles upsert + tombstone deletes.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Search Index: Sharded inverted + geo index with alias-swapped versions for blue/green reindexing.

nodes.label: Query / Detail Cache
nodes.description: Query / Detail Cache: Caches hot search results and listing-detail JSON for the seeker read path.

nodes.label: Identity & Access Mgmt
nodes.description: Identity & Access Mgmt: OAuth2, API keys, RBAC, owner-tenant scoping, mTLS for partner systems.

nodes.label: Rate Limiter
nodes.description: Rate Limiter: Per-owner / per-API-key quotas, burst control, abuse protection (state in RateLimitStore).

nodes.label: Webhook Dispatcher
nodes.description: Webhook Dispatcher: Signs and retries owner callbacks (import-completion, listing-status); DLQ on permanent failure.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Notification Service: Owner email/in-app alerts on validation errors, fraud holds, expiry; also delivers seeker saved-search alerts.

nodes.label: Analytics Pipeline
nodes.description: Analytics Pipeline: Captures seeker events (view/click/contact), feeds dashboards and ranking signals — derived projection only.

nodes.label: Observability (metrics/logs/traces)
nodes.description: Observability (metrics/logs/traces): Dashboards for indexing lag, import jobs, fraud decisions, DLQ depth, API per-owner usage, moderation backlog.

nodes.label: Promotion Service
nodes.description: Promotion Service: returns active promotion boost per listing_id; consulted by the indexer (and optionally the ranker) so paid boost is one capped term in the ranking formula, not a master switch.

nodes.label: Promotion Store
nodes.description: Promotion Store: listing_promotions (purchased boost orders) + promotion_events (impressions/clicks for billing + pacing).

nodes.label: Billing / Ad-Spend Service
nodes.description: Billing / Ad-Spend Service: charges owners per impression/click against a daily/total budget; tells PromotionSvc when budget is exhausted so the boost goes inactive.

nodes.label: Media CDN (separate origin)
nodes.description: Media CDN (separate origin): Edge cache fronting MediaStore on its own hostname (e.g. img.example.com), cookie-less, with immutable long-TTL caching. Distinct from the API CDN so media bandwidth, cache policy, and failure domain are isolated from search/detail traffic.

capacityDiagram: Pipeline[Validate / Normalize / Enrich / Dedup / Fraud / Media]

functional: Ingest listings via SFTP (CSV/XML/JSON, possibly ZIP/GZIP), incrementally or in bulk.
Ingest listings via authenticated REST API: create, update, partial-update, delete/unpublish, bulk.
Manual + AI-assisted listing authoring in the admin panel — suggestions never auto-publish.
Validate, normalize, and enrich listings (geocoding, classification, quality/completeness, media) before publication.
Process media safely: fetch, virus-scan, strip EXIF/PII, transcode, thumbnail, and moderate (NSFW + image-reuse).
Detect exact + near-duplicate listings across owners and channels before they reach search.
Score every listing for fraud; auto-publish below threshold, route flagged ones to a moderator queue.
Track full listing lifecycle (draft, validated, enriched, fraud_review, published, paused, expired, rejected, deleted) with version ordering and tombstone propagation.
Maintain a near-real-time search index supporting keyword, filter, geo, and ranking (relevance + freshness + quality + owner reputation; hide duplicates and fraud).
Serve seekers on a public website and mobile app: search, detail pages, saved listings, saved searches + alerts, report-listing.
Admin panel for owners (their listings + imports + analytics) and platform admins (moderation, taxonomy, RBAC, audits).
Owner-facing import status, per-row error reports, signed webhook callbacks with retries.
nonFunctional: Public seeker search and listing detail are always-available: ingestion degradation must NOT take down the read surface.
Search index freshness ~5 minutes (a publish-to-visible target most listings beat).
Search query latency p95 < 300 ms; listing detail p95 < 200 ms.
Scale: 10M active / 500M historical listings, 50k owners, 5k FTP imports/day, ~100M API writes/month, 50M seeker searches/day.
At-least-once processing with idempotency at TWO layers: request idempotency (Idempotency-Key) AND logical-listing idempotency ((owner_id, external_listing_id) + version ordering).
Multi-tenant isolation: an owner's blast or bug can't starve other owners or break seeker reads.
Audit every state transition, fraud decision, dedup grouping, and admin action; support replay of failed processing jobs.
GDPR-style erasure across listings, media, search index, caches, feature store, and audit (tombstones may persist; PII/contact/media are erased or anonymized).
Webhook callbacks delivered at-least-once with HMAC signing, exponential backoff, and DLQ on permanent failure.

capacity.label: Active listings
capacity.note: Indexed + serveable; the working set for seeker traffic.

capacity.label: Listing owners
capacity.note: Tenant scope for partitioning, quotas, and RBAC.

capacity.label: FTP imports
capacity.note: Heavy tail: a single import can be 10M rows (~50 GB). Row count, not file count, drives pipeline load.

capacity.label: Ingestion writes
capacity.value: ~3-4k row events/s sustained; ~30k/s peak after one big FTP drop
capacity.note: Derived: 5k files/day x ~2k avg rows /86400s ≈ 100/s steady from files; 10M-row spikes dominate. API writes are ~40/s avg, ~400/s peak.

capacity.label: Pipeline work
capacity.note: Validate, normalize, enrich, dedup, fraud, index — each a partitioned worker pool.

capacity.label: Seeker searches
capacity.note: 50M/day. CDN + QueryCache absorb most; the cluster sees the long tail and the misses.

capacity.label: Seeker detail views
capacity.note: 5M/day. Listing-detail JSON is highly cacheable (changes rarely after publish).

capacity.label: Search index size
capacity.note: Active SearchDocs. Primary data is ~10-30 GB compressed; with analyzed fields, embeddings, replicas (~2x), and per-domain subfields the on-disk footprint is ~50-150 GB across ~20 primary shards. Refresh ~1s; index write QPS bounded by indexer batching.

capacity.label: Enrichment fan-out
capacity.note: Geocode (cached), classify, language detect, optional translate, quality/completeness. Cache hit ratio target >60% on geocode.

capacity.label: Media volume
capacity.note: Originals + ~3 thumb sizes ≈ 150M media objects. Storage ~tens of TB; CDN egress dominates cost.

capacity.label: Moderation queue
capacity.note: Fraud holds + suspected duplicates + seeker reports. Backlog SLO drives moderator headcount.

capacity.label: Webhook callbacks
capacity.note: At-least-once with retries; partners' downtime causes retry backlog — bounded by DLQ.

capacity.label: Search freshness
capacity.note: Most listings beat that; flagged-for-review may take hours; deletes propagate within seconds.

capacity.label: Active saved searches
capacity.note: Drives AlertWorker fan-out: every listing.published / .updated must be matched against active saved searches in the relevant category/geo. At this scale the matcher uses inverted indexing on the saved-search side (a 'reverse search engine'), not naive per-listing scans.

capacity.label: Promotion impressions / clicks
capacity.value: ~5-15% of seeker reads emit a promo event
capacity.note: At 50M searches/day with ~2 promoted slots per page, impression events are ~70-90M/day; clicks are typically 1-3% of impressions. promotion_events is a high-volume append stream (Kafka topic, not a relational table on the request path); BillingSvc consumes it asynchronously.

api.description: Create one listing. Idempotency-Key dedupes producer retries; the listing flows through the async pipeline before becoming visible. The optional `source_version` (or `external_updated_at`) lets the pipeline reject stale updates that arrive out of order.
api.request:
Idempotency-Key: req_42
Authorization: Bearer <token>
{
  "external_listing_id": "ext_9",
  "source_version": 12,
  "category": "jobs",
  "title": "Senior backend engineer",
  "description": "...",
  "location": "Berlin, DE",
  "salary": { "min": 80000, "currency": "EUR" },
  "media": [{ "url": "https://owner.cdn/img1.jpg" }]
}

api.description: Partial update; same async pipeline. Only the affected stages re-run (e.g. a price change re-indexes but doesn't re-enrich category). Returns a new correlation id for status polling. The optional `source_version` gates stale updates — if it's older than the listing's current version, the PATCH is rejected by the pipeline (not silently applied).

Unpublish + delete a listing. Emits `listing.deleted`; the indexer publishes a tombstone (delete from SearchIdx + invalidate caches). PII/contact and media are erased; metadata + audit may persist (GDPR-compliant tombstone).
Bulk upload up to N listings (mix of upserts and deletes). Always asynchronous — returns a job id the partner polls or gets via signed webhook.
Status of one import job: counts of total / succeeded / failed / duplicate, plus an error-report URL.
Current lifecycle state + last fraud / dedup / enrichment / media decision for one listing.
Public seeker search. Heavily cacheable (CDN + QueryCache). Cursor-based pagination (`search_after`) for stable results under changing indexes.
Seeker abuse report. Creates a `listing_reports` row and routes the listing to the moderator queue.
Persist a seeker's search filter and subscribe to alerts when matching listings publish or update.
Register an owner webhook endpoint with a shared HMAC secret. The dispatcher signs each callback and retries with exponential backoff.

dataModel.name: listings
dataModel.note: Source of truth. One row per (listing_id, version). Owner-provided fields are kept separable from enriched fields (each enriched field carries provenance via enrichment_results).

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(draft, validated, enriched, dup_suspected, fraud_review, approved, published, paused, expired, rejected, deleted)

fields.name: contact
fields.type: jsonb (encrypted at rest; erased on GDPR delete)

fields.name: tombstoned_at
fields.type: timestamptz null (GDPR-erased keeps metadata, drops PII)

dataModel.name: listing_state_transitions
dataModel.note: Append-only state-machine log: every transition with actor, reason, and version. Drives the audit log and the outbox.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Transactional outbox. Written in the same DB transaction as the listing change; the tail process publishes to the event bus. Trim policy: delete rows older than N days that all consumers have committed past. UNIQUE (aggregate_id, sequence) — enforces per-aggregate ordering on the producer side; consumers reject events with stale sequence.

fields.name: event_version
fields.type: int (schema version of this event type)

dataModel.name: listing_owners
dataModel.note: Tenant. Reputation, billing, and integration configuration live here.

fields.name: integrations
fields.type: jsonb (ftp config, api keys, webhook urls + HMAC secrets)

dataModel.name: import_jobs
dataModel.note: One row per FTP file or bulk-API submission. Counts roll up from import_records.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(received, parsing, validating, enriching, deduping, fraud_checking, indexed, partial_failed, failed)

dataModel.name: import_records
dataModel.note: Per-row outcome. Drives owner-facing error reports and partial-failure replay.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(ok, validation_failed, duplicate, fraud_held, media_blocked, rejected)

dataModel.name: enrichment_results
dataModel.note: Append-only history of enrichment outputs, separable from owner-provided fields. Carries model_version + confidence for backtest/rollback.

fields.name: enrichment_type
fields.type: enum(category, geocode, language, translate, quality, completeness, seo, embedding)

dataModel.name: fraud_decisions
dataModel.note: One row per scoring run. Explains why a listing was held / cleared and supports backtesting rule changes.

dataModel.name: duplicate_candidates
dataModel.note: Pairs of suspected duplicate listings. Resolved into duplicate_group_id on the listing. Invalidated on listing update (content_hash change triggers re-dedup).

dataModel.name: external_id_map
dataModel.note: (owner_id, external_listing_id) -> listing_id. The deterministic-dedup table; a unique key here lets the platform absorb idempotent re-imports cheaply. UNIQUE (owner_id, external_listing_id) — the constraint that makes repeated imports collapse to one listing.

dataModel.name: media_assets
dataModel.note: One row per immutable media object. Original URLs are fetched, scanned, normalized; derived variants (thumb_small, thumb_large) reference the same row. On GDPR erasure: original + thumbnails are deleted from MediaStore; deleted_at is set; the row is retained ~30 days for operational audit then hard-deleted.

fields.name: scan_status
fields.type: enum(pending, clean, infected, unsafe, copyright_block, fetch_failed)

fields.name: scan_signals
fields.type: jsonb (NSFW score, reuse fingerprint, EXIF removed flags)

dataModel.name: search_documents
dataModel.note: The projected SearchDoc shape (indexed in SearchIdx). Updated by the indexer from listings + enrichment + dedup + fraud state. Hidden/tombstoned when state ∉ {published}.

fields.name: fraud_status
fields.type: keyword (clean / held — held docs never indexed)

fields.name: duplicate_group_id
fields.type: keyword (only canonical of group is indexed)

fields.name: domain_fields
fields.type: object (jobs.salary_min, real_estate.bedrooms, vehicle.year — schema per category)

fields.name: promotion_boost
fields.type: float (capped ranking multiplier; 1.0 when no active promo)

dataModel.name: saved_searches
dataModel.note: Seeker subscriptions: persisted filter + alert config. The alert worker matches new/updated listings against these.

dataModel.name: saved_listings
dataModel.note: Seeker's bookmarked listings.

dataModel.name: listing_reports
dataModel.note: Seeker abuse reports. Each report routes the listing into ModQueue; multiple reports raise priority.

fields.name: reason
fields.type: enum(scam, spam, inappropriate, offensive, wrong_category, other)

dataModel.name: ai_authoring_events
dataModel.note: Every AI suggestion made + whether owner accepted/rejected it. Audits hallucinations and feeds prompt/model evaluation.

fields.name: suggestion_type
fields.type: enum(title, description, category, tags, translate, policy_check, normalize)

dataModel.name: rate_limit_state
dataModel.note: Per-owner token buckets and import-concurrency leases. Atomic increment+expire — typically Redis, not relational.

dataModel.name: webhook_deliveries
dataModel.note: Each callback attempt: ordered, signed, retried, dead-lettered. Lets owners see delivery history and replay. A per-delivery idempotency key (delivery_id) is sent in the callback header so owners can dedupe retries on their side.

dataModel.name: audit_log
dataModel.note: Every state change, admin action, override, and AI acceptance. Append-only / tamper-evident (hash-chained). Required for compliance and post-incident review. Retention: ~7 years (regulatory baseline for most listing domains); GDPR erasure anonymizes actor PII but preserves the hash chain so tamper-evidence survives.

fields.name: prev_hash
fields.type: varchar (chains to previous row for tamper-evidence)

dataModel.name: listing_promotions
dataModel.note: An owner's purchased boost for one listing: type, budget, schedule, and current state. Read by PromotionSvc; written by BillingSvc on purchase / pause / expiry.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(scheduled, active, paused, exhausted, expired, blocked)

dataModel.name: promotion_events
dataModel.note: Append-only impression/click STREAM (Kafka topic / object-storage log, NOT a relational table on the request path). BFF writes events to Analytics → BillingSvc consumes for pacing and ad-spend reconciliation. Volume: tens of millions/day; retained for ~90 days for billing audit then aggregated and pruned. Fraud detection on inflated CTR / self-clicks reads from this stream.

fields.name: query_hash
fields.type: varchar null (context: which search / page surfaced it)

technologyChoices.concern: Owner API gateway, auth, and rate limiting
tradeoff: Self-hosted gateways give full policy/plugin control and avoid per-request gateway pricing, but you own upgrades, config rollouts, and HA.
Managed API gateways and identity products reduce auth, throttling, and certificate operations, but can make advanced tenant-specific routing and custom rate-limit algorithms harder.
Rate limits still need a fast state store even when the gateway is managed; the gateway only enforces what the quota store can decide quickly.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed gateway with built-in auth, WAF, throttling, and usage plans removes much of the custom edge/auth code in step 2, but not the per-owner quota model.

technologyChoices.concern: Event bus and async processing pipeline
tradeoff: A log/stream (Kafka, Redpanda, Pulsar, Kinesis, Event Hubs) fits ordered lifecycle events, replay, and multiple consumer groups.
A work queue (RabbitMQ, SQS, Service Bus, Cloud Tasks) is simpler for discrete retries and DLQs, but weaker for long replay and multiple projections.
Managed streams reduce broker operations but introduce partition throughput limits, retention limits, and cloud-specific consumer semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, and native DLQ support removes much of the hand-built retry and offset bookkeeping from the ingestion pipeline.

technologyChoices.concern: Listing source of truth and transactional outbox
tradeoff: Relational stores are a strong fit for listing versions, state transitions, outbox rows, and audit queries because transactions matter.
Wide-column/NoSQL stores can absorb very high write rates and tenant skew, but make cross-row state transitions and outbox consistency more application-owned.
Distributed SQL reduces shard management and improves cross-region options, but adds cost and latency to hot write paths.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A database with CDC or change streams can remove a custom outbox tailer, but it does not remove the need for idempotent downstream consumers.

technologyChoices.concern: Raw file and media object storage
tradeoff: Object storage is the right primitive for raw FTP files, immutable media originals, generated thumbnails, and replay/audit retention.
Self-hosting gives storage-cost control at very high volume but pushes durability, lifecycle policies, and multi-region replication onto the team.
Cloud object stores plus CDN reduce durability and edge-delivery work, but media egress and derivative explosion become the main cost risks.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed object lifecycle rules can replace custom cleanup jobs for raw-file retention, thumbnail variants, and archived historical media.

tradeoff: Self-hosting image/media workers gives full codec and scanner control but needs careful sandboxing for untrusted files.
Serverless workers are convenient for bursty thumbnailing/scanning, but large videos, long scans, and high concurrency can hit timeout and cost limits.
Managed image moderation helps bootstrap safety but still needs human-review escalation and false-positive monitoring.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed scan/transform pipeline can remove custom thumbnail workers for standard images, but not the policy decisions around what blocks publication.

tradeoff: Search engines are optimized for keyword, facet, geo, and ranking workloads that relational databases should not serve directly.
Self-hosted search gives low-level shard/refresh/ranking control but creates a serious operations surface around reindexing and cluster recovery.
Managed search reduces cluster operations but can limit custom ranking, vector features, and low-level index tuning.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed search service with aliases and snapshots removes much of the search-cluster operations burden, but not the need for blue/green reindex and version-conditional updates.

tradeoff: Dedicated vector databases simplify ANN search, metadata filters, and online index updates for fuzzy dedup.
Embedding search inside the main search engine reduces moving parts but can couple dedup tuning to seeker-search cluster capacity.
A local library such as Faiss is fast and cheap for batch/rebuild workflows, but online serving, replication, and filtering become application concerns.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed vector index can remove custom ANN service operations, but not the need for domain-specific blocking keys and reversible duplicate decisions.

technologyChoices.concern: Fraud feature store and rules engine
tradeoff: A feature store gives low-latency, training/serving-consistent fraud features, but adds data freshness and backfill complexity.
A simple rules engine is easier for trust-and-safety operators to control than pure ML, but rule sprawl needs versioning and rollout discipline.
Cloud serverless rules are quick to change, but high-QPS scoring usually needs precomputed features and warm capacity.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed feature store or durable KV with TTL counters removes many custom rolling-window feature jobs, but not the human review loop.

tradeoff: CDN caching is best for anonymous search/detail responses and static media; query caches are better for repeated but not globally-cacheable searches.
Self-hosted caches are cheaper and more tunable close to the BFF, but do not replace global edge caching.
Personalization, saved listings, and user-specific promotion targeting must stay out of shared CDN keys.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A CDN with stale-while-revalidate and tag purge support removes most custom edge-cache refresh logic, but not BFF-level personalization boundaries.

tradeoff: Workflow engines make retry, timeout, backoff, and replay explicit, which is useful for partner webhooks and import completion.
Simple queue workers are easier to operate at first, but replay UI, per-destination backoff, and long-running callbacks become custom code.
Managed workflow products reduce orchestration code, but per-state-transition cost can surprise at high callback volume.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed workflow/queue with per-message retry and DLQ support removes a custom webhook scheduler, but not HMAC signing or partner-facing delivery history.

tradeoff: Promotion impressions/clicks are analytics events first and billing inputs second; they should not block seeker search.
A columnar analytics store is better for reporting and pacing metrics than a relational OLTP table at 50M searches/day.
Payment providers solve charging and refunds, but not ranking fairness, click-fraud signals, or budget-exhaustion propagation.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed event/analytics pipeline removes custom batch ETL for promotion events, but not the reconciliation between metered events, owner invoices, and SearchDoc boost state.

tradeoff: OpenTelemetry keeps traces/metrics/logs portable across clouds and self-hosted services, but still needs a backend and sampling discipline.
Managed observability reduces storage and dashboard operations, but costs can spike from high-cardinality per-owner labels.
The key SLOs are product-specific: import lag, publish-to-visible lag, media scan lag, moderation backlog, webhook failure rate, and promotion exhaustion lag.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed telemetry backends remove most metric/log storage operations, but not the need to define per-tenant SLOs and alert ownership.

patterns.name: Async pipeline on a partitioned event bus
patterns.what: Each pipeline stage is its own consumer group on a log partitioned by listing_id; stages scale and fail independently.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-stage processing where latency budget per write is seconds-to-minutes and stages have different cost/SLOs.

patterns.name: Outbox + CDC
patterns.what: Write the listing row and an outbox row in one transaction; a tail process publishes outbox rows to the event bus.
patterns.whenToUse: When downstream projections (search index, analytics) must never miss a state change, even if a producer crashes.

patterns.name: Idempotency key + deterministic dedup table
patterns.what: Per-owner unique (owner_id, external_listing_id) absorbs repeated imports of the same logical listing.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same listing arrives over FTP today and API tomorrow, or producers retry on timeouts.

patterns.name: Versioned events with source_version ordering
patterns.what: Each lifecycle event carries (aggregate_id, sequence, event_version). Consumers reject stale updates by sequence; new schema versions land behind feature flags.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-channel ingestion (FTP + API + manual) where old and new updates can race.

patterns.name: Blocking + ANN for fuzzy dedup
patterns.what: Reduce O(N^2) comparisons by blocking on cheap keys (category + city + price band), then near-neighbor search in an embedding/MinHash index.

patterns.name: Rules + ML fraud scoring with human-in-the-loop
patterns.what: Hot-reloadable rule packs as a cheap filter; ML model contributes a probabilistic score; borderline → moderator queue; decisions feed back.
patterns.whenToUse: When you need fast rule rollouts AND ML quality; pure ML is too slow to iterate on emerging abuse.

patterns.name: Source-of-truth + projected search index with tombstones
patterns.what: Listing DB is authoritative; SearchIdx is a derived projection. Deletes propagate as explicit tombstones; reindex via blue/green alias swap.
patterns.whenToUse: When search shape changes more often than the truth, or a bad indexer needs a clean recovery path.

patterns.name: Media safety pipeline
patterns.what: Fetch owner-provided URLs into immutable storage; virus-scan, EXIF-strip, transcode, classify (NSFW/reuse), thumbnail. Block-publish-or-partial policy per scan result.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever owners can supply external media URLs that become public.

patterns.name: CDN + cache-aside read path
patterns.what: Public search and detail responses are CDN-edge cached; misses go through a query cache, then the search index / DB. Stale-while-revalidate keeps reads up under index lag.
patterns.whenToUse: When read traffic is orders of magnitude larger than writes and most queries are repeats.

patterns.name: Per-tenant partitioning + fair scheduling
patterns.what: Partition the event bus by owner_id so one noisy owner can't starve others; a fair scheduler weights consumer capacity across tenants; hot-partition split on a dominant tenant.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tenant write pipelines with skewed owner sizes.

patterns.name: Webhook delivery as a distributed system
patterns.what: Signed callbacks, exponential-backoff retry, attempt log, DLQ on permanent failure, replay UI for owners.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever the platform notifies external systems asynchronously.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Synchronous Create-and-Index (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that could possibly work: the partner POSTs a listing, the API validates it, runs enrichment + fraud + dedup inline, writes the listing row, and pushes a document into the search index — all in one synchronous request before returning 201.

It's correct and easy to reason about, and it exposes every pressure this design needs to absorb: a slow ML model or geocoder couples directly to ingestion latency, a 10M-row FTP file is impossible because we have no async path, an indexer outage takes down writes, a single noisy partner saturates the API for everyone — and crucially, the naive design has no consistent story for updates, deletes, or out-of-order events. A PATCH that races a re-import of yesterday's file can silently roll back a price change.
Every later step removes one of these couplings.

view.caption: Everything happens inline: validate, score, dedup, store image bytes, write, index — then return. Even at this baseline, media has to live somewhere (MediaStore); the naive design just stores bytes synchronously, with no scan, no transcode, and no separate origin.

steps.decisionPrompt: If geocoding takes 800ms and ML fraud scoring takes 200ms, what is your minimum POST latency — and what happens when either is unavailable, or when a stale update arrives after a newer one?
whyNow: A concrete baseline makes the rest of the design earn its complexity step-by-step.

concepts.term: Coupling vs cohesion at the request boundary
concepts.definition: Whether the caller's success depends on every downstream succeeding right now, or just on the system accepting the work for eventual processing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Synchronous create-and-index couples partner availability to your slowest pipeline stage. The next step decouples it.

concepts.definition: Without a version or sequence stamped on each event, an out-of-order PATCH or DELETE can clobber a newer write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Listing platforms are mostly update churn; getting ordering wrong is the most common silent data bug.

concepts.term: Media needs storage from day one
concepts.definition: Even the naive design has to put image bytes somewhere durable — the listing row can't carry them inline. Object storage (MediaStore) is the right fit for any version of this system: cheap, immutable, scales independently. What changes across later steps is HOW bytes get there (URL-async vs sync-upload), what runs on them (virus scan, EXIF strip, transcode, thumbnails), and how they're served (direct vs through a separate media CDN).
concepts.whyItMatters: Recognizing MediaStore as a day-one component avoids the cargo-cult trap of 'media is a step-4 problem'. The storage choice is fixed early; only the surrounding pipeline grows.

traps.trap: Shipping the naive design and adding async 'later'
traps.why: FTP imports of 10M rows are simply not possible synchronously — the architecture must be async from day one.
traps.instead: Treat the API as 'accept fast, process async' from the start; every later step is just adding pipeline stages.

traps.trap: Treating create as the only verb
traps.why: Real listings change price hourly, get paused, expire, are deleted under GDPR, and re-emerge after re-import. Without explicit update/delete/ordering, the index drifts from truth.
traps.instead: Design lifecycle events from step 2 onward: upsert, unpublish, delete, restore — all carrying a version.

traps.trap: Storing media as DB blobs or in the listing row
traps.why: Listing rows become huge (slow reads, slow replication, slow backups); the DB becomes the CDN; row updates rewrite image bytes; and you'll regret it the day you need a thumbnail.
traps.instead: Object storage (MediaStore) from the start — the listing row keeps a media_id reference, the bytes live elsewhere. Later steps add the fetch/scan worker, the perceptual-hash index, and the separate media CDN, but the storage tier is correct from step 1.

recap.after: End-to-end create works, in one request.
recap.newRisk: Latency, availability, throughput, AND lifecycle correctness all collapse the moment a downstream is slow, a file is large, or events race.

steps.title: 2. API Ingestion: Accept Fast, Publish Versioned Events
description: Turn the API into a thin accept layer. The Owner API authenticates (OAuth2 / API key / mTLS), rate-limits per owner (counters in a fast `RateLimitStore`), validates the request shape, claims an idempotency key, and publishes a versioned `listing.upserted` / `.deleted` event to a partitioned log — returning 202 immediately with a correlation id.

Each event carries a monotonic `sequence` per `listing_id` plus an `event_version` schema tag. Downstream consumers reject stale events (`incoming.sequence <= committed.sequence`) and run consumer-compatibility code paths per `event_version`. This is what makes updates and deletes safe across re-imports, retries, and producer races.

Idempotency-Key on POST + (owner_id, external_listing_id) lookup on the consumer side ensures retries don't create duplicate listings. Webhook registration lives in `listing_owners.integrations`; the dispatcher signs every callback with HMAC and retries with exponential backoff (with a DLQ).

view.caption: The API authenticates, validates, and idempotently publishes one versioned event per logical write. Rate-limit state in a fast counter store; webhook delivery is its own retried subsystem with a DLQ.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the smallest unit of work the API has to do synchronously, and how do you ensure a stale PATCH that arrives after a newer one doesn't overwrite it?
patterns: Webhook delivery as a distributed system

concepts.definition: The Idempotency-Key header dedupes producer retries of the same HTTP request; the (owner_id, external_listing_id) tuple dedupes the underlying logical listing across requests/channels. Idempotency-Key state lives in the `RateLimitStore` (or a sibling KV) with a TTL of a few hours.
concepts.whyItMatters: Producers retry on network errors; partners re-upload yesterday's full file 'just to be safe'. Both must collapse to one listing — and the two key kinds collapse different problems.

concepts.definition: Token-bucket counters keyed by owner_id (or API key) capping writes/sec and concurrent in-flight imports. State lives in the `RateLimitStore` so multiple API instances share the same counters.
concepts.whyItMatters: Stateless rate limiting per instance lets a partner exceed quota by fanning across instances. Shared state is the difference between a quota and a suggestion.

concepts.definition: Every published event carries (aggregate_id, sequence, event_version). Consumers commit the last applied sequence per aggregate and reject older ones; new event schemas land behind versioned consumers for replay-safety.
concepts.whyItMatters: Listing churn is mostly updates; without ordering, a stale FTP re-import can silently roll back a fresh PATCH.

concepts.definition: An id the API returns and stamps on every downstream log/metric for this write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, debugging an async pipeline is impossible — partners report 'my POST cor_77 never got published' and you grep all stages.

concepts.term: Webhook delivery as a distributed system
concepts.definition: Each owner webhook is a destination; callbacks are signed (HMAC), tracked per attempt in `webhook_deliveries`, retried with exponential backoff, and dead-lettered on permanent failure (5xx run or 4xx no-retry).
concepts.whyItMatters: Owners' endpoints fail; without retries + replay UI, partners blame the platform for missed events that they actually rejected.

concepts.term: Media submitted by URL, never as bytes on this API
concepts.definition: Owners send media as URLs (HTTPS) in the listing payload — never raw image bytes through the create/update API. The actual fetch + scan + transcode happens asynchronously in step 4's media pipeline. The API validates URL shape, size hints (Content-Length probe), and reasonable count (e.g. ≤30 images/listing).
concepts.whyItMatters: Accepting binary uploads on the create endpoint couples the partner's request latency to media fetch + scan time, makes the request payload 1000x larger, and gives the API surface a malware-upload vector. URL submission keeps the create API tiny and pushes the heavy lifting to the right tier.

whyNow: Decoupling the API from processing — and stamping every event with a version — is the foundation everything else depends on.

flows.name: API accept + idempotent versioned publish
flows.note: Idempotency-Key state and per-owner counters share one fast KV. Events carry sequence + version for downstream ordering and schema evolution.

messages.label: publish listing.upserted seq=N v=1 (partition by owner_id)

strong: Separates accept from process and returns a correlation id.
Uses BOTH an idempotency key (producer retries) and a deterministic dedup tuple (logical listing).
Stamps every event with a sequence + event_version; rate-limit + idempotency state is explicit and shared.
Partitions the bus by owner_id so per-owner ordering is preserved.
weak: Runs validation 'after the queue' — schema/shape errors should still 4xx synchronously.
Uses only an idempotency key, missing the (owner_id, external_id) dedup that catches FTP+API double-ingest.
Stateless rate limiting (per-instance counters) that partners trivially bypass.

traps.trap: Returning 200 OK with a fake 'created' status
traps.why: The listing isn't visible yet; partners think it's live and ask why search doesn't return it.
traps.instead: Return 202 Accepted with a correlation id + status endpoint, so partners can poll or wait on a webhook.

traps.trap: Unversioned events
traps.why: First schema change breaks replay; a stale update silently overwrites a newer one.
traps.instead: Stamp event_version + per-aggregate sequence on every event from day one.

traps.trap: Accepting raw image bytes on the create/update API
traps.why: The API now has to allocate gigabytes of memory per partner burst, carry a malware-upload attack surface, and synchronously couple ingestion latency to media-scan time.
traps.instead: API accepts URLs only; MediaWorker fetches them asynchronously, scans, and stores. If owners need to upload bytes from a browser, issue them a presigned upload URL to MediaStore directly — never proxy bytes through the create API.

recap.before: Everything inline on the request path.
recap.after: API accepts fast, idempotently, and with versioned events; processing happens behind the bus; webhook delivery is a real subsystem.
recap.newRisk: The pipeline stages downstream of the bus now need to exist — and not lose messages or apply them out of order.

steps.title: 3. FTP Ingestion: Stream Large Files, Don't Load Them
description: Partners drop files into per-owner SFTP directories (per-owner credentials, rotatable, isolated). A file watcher detects new arrivals, content-hashes the file (skip duplicate uploads), registers an `import_jobs` row with `import_mode` (full snapshot vs incremental), copies the raw file into object storage (the audit trail), and emits `import.received`.

A streaming parser reads the file row-by-row from object storage — never into memory — handling compression (ZIP/GZIP) transparently. Each row becomes one `row.parsed` event on the same event bus the API uses. Malformed rows go to a DLQ with the row number + raw bytes; malformed files (bad header, broken archive) fail the whole job and notify the owner.

Full snapshot semantics: any external_listing_id NOT present in this snapshot is `expired` (configurable per owner — expire vs leave). Incremental semantics: only listings present in this file change. Getting this owner-config wrong is how a single bad re-upload accidentally expires a partner's whole catalog.
Per-owner import concurrency (lease in `RateLimitStore`) caps how many of an owner's files can parse at once — one 10M-row file from owner A shouldn't lock out parsing for owner B's small file.

view.caption: Files land in object storage; a streaming parser fans them into row events on the same bus the API uses. Full-snapshot vs incremental mode is owner config; per-owner concurrency caps prevent one drop from owning all parser capacity.

steps.decisionPrompt: A partner uploads a 10M-row, 4 GB CSV at the same minute another partner's smaller file arrives. Where do they live, and how do you keep both moving?

concepts.definition: Files land in object storage by content hash; an identical re-upload short-circuits (same file_hash → skip parsing). Originals are retained for replay and audit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Partners re-upload 'just to be safe' constantly. Recognizing identical bytes avoids redoing the whole pipeline.

concepts.definition: The pipeline's unit of work is one row, not one file. Failed rows DLQ; succeeded rows flow through.
concepts.whyItMatters: Partial failures shouldn't block successful rows — owners want 99,840 of 100,000 published, not zero.

concepts.definition: Owner config declares whether a file is a full snapshot (missing listings get expired/paused/left — owner choice) or a delta (only present rows change). The import_mode is stamped on each row event.
concepts.whyItMatters: Same file shape, two very different behaviors — getting this wrong silently expires a partner's whole catalog.

concepts.definition: A lease in the `RateLimitStore` caps how many of an owner's files can parse concurrently; new files queue (per-owner FIFO).
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, one owner's 10M-row drop monopolizes parser workers and stalls every other owner's small file.

concepts.definition: ZIP/GZIP streams transparently; broken archives fail the whole job (DLQ + notify) — never silently parse a truncated file.
concepts.whyItMatters: Partial archives produce phantom 'deletions' that look like data loss in full-snapshot mode.

concepts.term: Media URLs ride per-row through the same pipeline
concepts.definition: FTP rows carry the same `media: [{url}]` field as API requests. The parser doesn't fetch images; it emits the URLs in the row event and the step 4 media pipeline handles fetch + scan + transcode. FTP file size is bounded by row+text, not by media bytes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeping image bytes out of the FTP file (even a 10M-row file stays tens of GB, not multi-terabyte). It also means owners can host their own image CDNs and the platform doesn't need to know how partners manage their media.

whyNow: FTP is the bulk path; once API is async, FTP is 'turn each row into the same event'.

flows.name: Streaming file ingestion
flows.note: Content-hash check absorbs identical re-uploads. One file → N row events. The parser never holds the whole file in memory.

options.name: Streaming parser (default)
pros: Bounded memory regardless of file size — a 10M-row file is the same shape as a 100-row file.
Per-row failures don't poison the rest of the job.
Same row event as the API path: one downstream pipeline, two ingress channels.
cons: More moving parts than 'load and process'.
Per-row event overhead at small file sizes.

view.caption: FTP file -> object storage -> streaming parser fans rows onto the bus; per-row failures DLQ.

options.name: Load entire file, process in memory
pros: Simplest possible parser code.
cons: OOMs on large files; entire job retries the whole file.
No partial progress — 9,999,999 successful rows are lost if row 10M fails.

view.caption: File parsed in one process and emitted as events — simplest code, OOMs on 10M-row files.

failureDrills.scenario: Partner re-uploads yesterday's full file with no changes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: File-hash check short-circuits before parsing; zero new pipeline work.
failureDrills.mitigation: Content-hash on the file + (owner_id, external_id) deterministic dedup on each row absorbs both layers.

failureDrills.scenario: Parser crashes mid-file after 8M of 10M rows.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Parser resumes from a checkpoint (row offset) on restart; previously emitted rows are idempotent.
failureDrills.mitigation: Parser commits a row-offset checkpoint per N rows; on restart it re-reads from the offset and emits — downstream dedup + sequence ordering absorb the overlap.

failureDrills.scenario: Owner re-uploads a truncated full-snapshot file by accident.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Job fails (broken archive / unexpected EOF) and notifies owner; no listings are expired.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reject incomplete archives; never apply full-snapshot expiration on a failed parse.

strong: Streams from object storage, never loads the file into memory.
Treats the row as the unit of work and routes per-row failures to a DLQ.
Uses the SAME event bus + dedup tuple as the API path — one pipeline, two ingress channels.
Distinguishes full vs incremental import semantics and per-owner import concurrency.
weak: Reads the whole file in one process and 'retries the file' on partial failure.
Builds a separate FTP-only pipeline parallel to the API pipeline.
Has no story for what 'missing listings' means in a full-snapshot file.

traps.trap: Letting one bad row fail the whole job
traps.why: Partners want partial success — a typo in row 7 shouldn't block rows 8 through 10M.
traps.instead: DLQ the bad row, surface it in the per-row error report, and keep going (unless the owner asked for all-or-nothing).

traps.trap: Applying full-snapshot expiration on a partial file
traps.why: A truncated upload looks like 'these listings are gone' — and silently expires a partner's whole catalog.
traps.instead: Reject incomplete files outright; full-snapshot expiration is only valid after a confirmed-complete parse.

recap.after: Bulk FTP at any file size, full-snapshot vs incremental semantics explicit, per-owner concurrency capped — joining the same row-event pipeline as the API.
recap.newRisk: Bus throughput now matters; downstream stages must absorb peaks from one 10M-row drop.

steps.title: 4. Async Pipeline: Normalize → Enrich → Media → Persist
description: Each pipeline stage is its own consumer group on the event bus, partitioned by listing_id. The Normalizer canonicalizes fields (currency, units, owner taxonomy → ours) and writes the first listing row in state `validated`. The Enrichment orchestrator then calls geocoding, classification, language detection, translation, and quality scoring, writing each result into `enrichment_results` and updating the listing.

In parallel, the Media Worker fetches owner-supplied media URLs into immutable storage, runs them through the Media Scanner (virus scan, EXIF/PII strip, NSFW classifier, image-reuse fingerprint), transcodes, and generates thumbnails. The owner's policy decides whether media failure blocks publication or allows partial publish with `media_blocked` flags.

Owner-provided fields and enrichment outputs are kept separable — the listing schema marks every enriched field with its source and model version, so a bad model rollout never silently overwrites partner data.

AI-assisted authoring (in the admin panel) uses the same `ModelSvc` but in interactive mode: suggestions are never auto-published — they're written to `ai_authoring_events` with prompt + model version + input hash, and only become listing content when an owner explicitly accepts them. PII (contact fields) is redacted before any LLM call; cached suggestions by content_hash limit cost.
Enrichment failures are classified: transient (retry with backoff), permanent (DLQ + notify owner), or non-blocking (publish anyway with `enrichment.partial`).

view.caption: Three independent stages compose on the bus: normalize, enrich, and media. Each writes its own slice with provenance; AI authoring suggestions live in their own log and never auto-publish.

steps.decisionPrompt: Geocoding takes 600ms p95, the ML category model takes 200ms, and media scanning a single image takes 2s. Do you run them in parallel, sequentially, or batch them — and which can block publication?
patterns: Async pipeline on a partitioned event bus

concepts.term: Pipeline stage as a consumer group
concepts.definition: Each stage subscribes to the bus and emits its own output topic; stages scale workers independently based on their lag.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enrichment is much slower than normalization. If they shared a worker pool, the slow stage would starve the fast one.

concepts.definition: Every enriched field carries (source = enricher, model_version, confidence, enriched_at).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you A/B model versions, reprocess after a model bug, and never accidentally overwrite an owner's value with a model guess.

concepts.definition: Fetch URL → virus scan → EXIF strip → NSFW + reuse classify → transcode → thumbnail → immutable store. Per-image scan_status drives publication and search filters.
concepts.whyItMatters: Media is a security boundary: malware, illegal content, copyright reuse, and stripped PII all matter. A single 'box on a diagram' is not a design.

concepts.definition: AI suggestions are persisted to `ai_authoring_events` with prompt/model version and input hash; an owner or admin must explicitly accept before content becomes part of the listing.
concepts.whyItMatters: A hallucinated salary that goes live is a real lawsuit. Auditability + acceptance gate are the trust boundary.

concepts.definition: Transient retries with backoff; permanent dead-letters with a reason; non-blocking proceeds with partial enrichment and logs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without this split, a flaky geocoder either takes down ingestion (over-retrying) or silently kills listings (DLQ everything).

concepts.definition: Each event has `event_version`; consumers handle multiple versions during rollout and replay; deprecated versions get a sunset window before removal.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adding a field to `listing.enriched` should never break the indexer two days into a replay.

whyNow: Once events flow, the real value-add work — enrichment, media, AI authoring — gets its own scaling and failure budgets.

deepDives.title: Enrichment orchestration
points: Plan: a config-driven DAG of (geocode → classify → translate → quality) per listing type.
Parallelism: run independent enrichers in parallel; serial only when one feeds another (translation depends on language detection).
Reprocessing: when a model ships, kick off a backfill consumer that re-emits affected listings into the enrichment topic without disturbing live writes.
Cost control: cache geocoding by normalized address; cache LLM outputs by content hash; degrade gracefully when budgets exceeded.

deepDives.title: Media safety pipeline
points: Fetch with timeout + size cap; reject obvious abuse (oversized, wrong mime).
Virus scan (ClamAV-style) gates everything else; infected files are quarantined + alert.
EXIF/GPS/PII strip is mandatory — owner-uploaded photos can leak home addresses.
NSFW + reuse classifier from `ModelSvc`; image-reuse fingerprint feeds the fraud feature store.
Transcode + thumbnail variants; originals are immutable, derived can be regenerated.
Per-listing policy: 'all media must pass' (jobs) vs 'partial publish OK' (marketplace).

deepDives.title: AI-assisted authoring (admin panel)
points: Interactive AI calls stream tokens with low latency budgets; async enrichment batches.
PII redaction before LLM: replace contact fields with placeholders, send only owner-safe text.
Suggestions persist with prompt_version + model_version + input_hash for replay/audit.
Acceptance flips a field with provenance (source=ai_assisted); never auto-published.
Cache by input_hash: identical descriptions don't re-call the model; vendor timeout degrades to manual.

deepDives.title: Event schema evolution
points: Every event carries `event_version`. Producers bump on additive change; consumers handle N and N-1.
Breaking changes go through a dual-write window: producers emit both v1 + v2 until v1 consumers drain.
Replay tooling pins the consumer to a specific event_version range so a backfill behaves like the original write.

options.name: Orchestrator with parallel sub-calls (default)
pros: Latency = max(enricher latencies), not sum.
Clear failure classification per enricher.
Easy to add/remove enrichers via config.
cons: Orchestrator becomes a coordination hotspot.
Partial-failure semantics need explicit policy per enricher.

view.caption: An Enricher orchestrator calls geocoding and ML in parallel and writes the listing row — latency = max(enricher), not sum.

options.name: Choreography: each enricher subscribes independently
pros: No orchestrator; enrichers scale fully independently.
cons: Race conditions writing the same listing row from N stages.
Hard to know when 'enrichment is done' (no single completion signal).

view.caption: Each enricher subscribes to the same topic and writes the listing row directly — no orchestrator, but races on the row + no single 'done' signal.

strong: Separates pipeline stages so each scales / fails independently.
Keeps owner-provided fields distinct from enrichment outputs with provenance.
Has an explicit policy for each enrichment failure class.
Treats media as a real safety pipeline, not a single box.
AI suggestions are logged + acceptance-gated, not auto-applied.
weak: Writes enrichment results back into owner-provided fields with no provenance.
Treats every enrichment failure the same way (always DLQ, or always retry forever).
Media is 'just storage'; no scan or strip.
AI calls go directly to the LLM with PII, and outputs auto-overwrite owner content.

traps.trap: Sequential enrichment so 'enrich' becomes one long step
traps.why: Latency = sum of all enricher latencies; one slow vendor stalls every listing.
traps.instead: Parallel calls when independent; serial only on real data dependencies.

traps.trap: No provenance on enriched fields
traps.why: A bad model rollout silently overwrites owner data; rolling back means restoring from backups.
traps.instead: Mark every enriched field with model_version + source; rollback is 'reprocess with the previous version'.

traps.trap: Auto-publishing AI suggestions
traps.why: Hallucinated salaries, made-up amenities, mistranslated category — all become legal/trust problems.
traps.instead: AI suggestions persist in `ai_authoring_events`; owner must accept; every acceptance audited.

recap.before: Row events with nothing acting on them.
recap.after: A normalized + enriched listing row, safe-processed media refs, AI-suggestion log — all with provenance and a CDC outbox.
recap.newRisk: Duplicates haven't been merged yet, and nothing has scored these for fraud.

steps.title: 5. Duplicate Detection: Exact First, Fuzzy Second
description: Two layers of deduplication run before publication. Exact dedup is a unique-key lookup in the external-ID map keyed by `(owner_id, external_listing_id)` — this absorbs the most common case (a partner re-pushes the same listing from FTP and API) at lookup cost.

Fuzzy dedup catches the harder cases: a listing posted by two different owners, a reposted listing with minor edits, the same job description from an aggregator. A content hash on normalized fields blocks the obvious clones; a vector embedding + ANN index catches near-duplicates without N-squared comparisons. Blocking keys are domain-specific: jobs block on (category, city); real estate on (city, bedrooms, price_band); vehicles on (make, model, year, location). The right blocking keys are the difference between 'fuzzy dedup at scale' and 'fuzzy dedup as a thought experiment'.

Both layers feed a moderator review queue when confidence is borderline. Merge decisions are auditable and reversible: the system writes a `duplicate_group_id`, not a hard merge — the canonical of each group is what gets indexed; the rest are hidden but recoverable.

On listing update: a content_hash change invalidates the row's `duplicate_candidates` and triggers re-dedup. On listing delete: if the deleted listing was a group's canonical, the group's next-best becomes canonical and a SearchDoc upsert replaces it.

view.caption: Exact dedup is a hash lookup; fuzzy dedup combines text content hashes, embeddings, and PERCEPTUAL IMAGE HASHES (pHash from MediaScanner) in HashIdx with domain-specific blocking keys. Borderline matches route to a human queue; merges record a group, not a destructive merge.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop a 10M-listing fuzzy-dedup run from doing 10M × 10M comparisons, and how do you keep groupings correct as listings update and delete?

concepts.definition: Deterministic uses a stable per-owner external id (exact match, unique constraint). Fuzzy uses content signals (hashes, embeddings, geography) when there's no shared id.
concepts.whyItMatters: 90% of duplicates are caught by deterministic dedup at trivial cost. The fuzzy layer earns its complexity on the remaining hard cases.

concepts.definition: Reduce the comparison space by bucketing candidates on cheap keys before expensive similarity. The right keys depend on category: jobs ≈ (category, city); real estate ≈ (city, bedrooms, price_band); vehicles ≈ (make, model, year).
concepts.whyItMatters: Without domain-aware blocking, fuzzy dedup is O(N^2) and intractable. With it, it's O(N * avg_bucket_size).

concepts.definition: Listings keep their identity; the system groups duplicates by a shared id. The canonical of the group is indexed; the rest are hidden but recoverable. Updates and deletes can promote a new canonical.
concepts.whyItMatters: A false merge is unrecoverable if you actually overwrote the loser. Grouping makes 'oops, those weren't duplicates' a one-row update.

concepts.definition: When a listing's content_hash changes, its `duplicate_candidates` rows are invalidated and re-dedup is enqueued.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without this, an edit that fixes the listing leaves it stuck in a wrong group forever.

concepts.term: Perceptual image hashing as a dedup signal
concepts.definition: MediaScanner computes a perceptual hash (pHash / dHash) of each image and stores it alongside the content/embedding signals in HashIdx. Two listings whose images all match perceptually are very likely the same underlying listing — even if the title and description are rewritten.
concepts.whyItMatters: Text dedup misses the most common cross-owner case: a scraper or scam reposts the same listing with rewritten copy. Image hashes catch it because rewriting the description is cheap but generating different real photos is expensive.

whyNow: Dedup must run BEFORE indexing — duplicates in search results are the most visible quality problem.

flows.name: Two-layer dedup
flows.note: Cheap deterministic first; fuzzy ANN with domain-specific blocking second; humans only on borderline.

strong: Exact dedup with a unique key on (owner_id, external_id) for idempotent re-imports.
Domain-specific blocking strategy before any embedding/ANN comparisons.
Reversible grouping (duplicate_group_id) with explicit canonical selection.
Invalidation + re-dedup on update; canonical promotion on delete.
Human-in-the-loop for borderline scores.
weak: Full O(N^2) similarity scan with no blocking.
Destructive merge with no audit / undo path.
Auto-merging across different owners without explicit policy.
Same blocking keys for jobs and real-estate as if domains don't matter.

traps.trap: Auto-merging duplicates across different owners
traps.why: Two real-estate agencies legitimately list the same building; merging deletes one owner's listing.
traps.instead: By default only auto-merge WITHIN an owner; cross-owner duplicates go to a moderator with both records visible.

traps.trap: Doing fuzzy dedup synchronously on every write
traps.why: An ANN lookup over 10M vectors is too slow for the write path; it'd dominate ingest latency.
traps.instead: Run fuzzy dedup as its own pipeline stage with its own backpressure — bounded latency, not bounded write latency.

traps.trap: Treating identical photos as proof of duplicate listings
traps.why: Two real-estate agencies legitimately use the same MLS photos; an OEM and its dealers all use the same product shots; many vehicles on a dealer floor get one stock image. Auto-merging on image match alone would destroy legitimate competing listings.
traps.instead: Use image match as ONE strong signal among (content_hash, embedding similarity, location, price) — not as a sole merge trigger. Cross-owner image matches especially go to a moderator.

failureDrills.scenario: A bug marks 1M listings as duplicates incorrectly.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reversible. Admin can bulk-clear duplicate_group_id and reprocess; nothing was destroyed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Grouping (not merging) + audit of every dedup decision; replay tooling that rewrites group ids based on a fresh dedup model.

failureDrills.scenario: A group's canonical listing is deleted.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Next-best member becomes canonical; SearchDoc upserted with the new canonical; old SearchDoc tombstoned.
failureDrills.mitigation: Canonical-selection rule is deterministic (e.g. highest quality_score, earliest published_at) and re-runs on group membership change.

recap.before: Duplicates leaked into the listing store.
recap.after: Same-owner re-imports collapse for free; cross-owner near-duplicates are grouped with domain-aware blocking, with humans for borderline; updates and deletes maintain group integrity.
recap.newRisk: Fraudulent listings still get through — dedup recognizes 'this is the same listing', not 'this is a scam'.

steps.title: 6. Fraud Prevention: Rules + ML, with a Human Loop
description: Every listing that survives dedup gets scored. A rules engine evaluates hot-deployable rule packs (banned phrases, regex, suspicious-IP, banned owner, payment scam phrases) first — cheap, explainable, and editable by trust-and-safety without a deploy. An ML scoring service then adds a probabilistic risk score using features from a feature store: owner reputation, recent listing churn, image-reuse signals (from the media pipeline), content embeddings.

Listings below a safe threshold auto-publish. Above a high threshold, auto-reject + notify owner. In the middle band, hold for moderator review with the explanation (which rules fired, which features contributed). Moderator decisions transition the listing state (`fraud_review` → `approved`/`rejected`), which writes a `listing_state_transitions` row and an outbox event — so the indexer publishes (or tombstones) the result without a separate code path. Moderator decisions also feed back into the ML training set and into owner-reputation features.

Every fraud decision is logged with model version and triggered rules so false-positive / false-negative rates can be measured per cohort. A moderation SLA (e.g. 24h p95 for held listings) drives queue alerting and moderator headcount.

view.caption: Cheap rules first, expensive ML second, humans last. FraudSvc reads image-reuse + NSFW signals from MediaScanner (a stolen-photo repost is one of the strongest fraud features). Moderator decisions flow back through the bus as listing.approved/.rejected — same indexing code path as any other lifecycle event.

steps.decisionPrompt: What do you do when the ML fraud service is unavailable? Block publication, or publish-and-flag?
patterns: Rules + ML fraud scoring with human-in-the-loop

concepts.definition: Rules are explicit, instant to deploy, fully explainable. ML is probabilistic, slower to iterate, but catches patterns rules can't enumerate. The platform combines both into one score.
concepts.whyItMatters: Pure ML is too slow to iterate against adversaries who change tactics weekly. Pure rules can't catch novel patterns. The combination wins.

concepts.definition: Pre-computed, low-latency features (owner reputation rolling avg, recent listing churn, image-reuse fingerprint hits, content embeddings) read by the scoring service at sub-10ms.
concepts.whyItMatters: ML scoring needs features fast; computing them in the request path means seconds-per-listing latency.

concepts.definition: Every score persists with the rules that fired, the model version, and the features that contributed most.
concepts.whyItMatters: Owners ask 'why was my listing held?'; auditors require it; ML training needs labeled data — explanation handles all three.

concepts.term: Moderator decision as a lifecycle event
concepts.definition: Approve / reject in ModQueue writes a state transition + outbox event (`listing.approved` / `listing.rejected`). Same downstream path as any other lifecycle change.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, you build a parallel 'moderation → indexer' path that drifts from the main pipeline.

concepts.definition: When fraud service is unavailable, default behavior: publish low-risk owners (high reputation), hold the rest. Documented and tunable.
concepts.whyItMatters: An adversary could deliberately overload the scorer to bypass it; the fallback must not be 'open the floodgates'.

concepts.definition: Time-to-decision p95 on held listings; backlog depth per owner / category. Drives alerting and staffing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, owners' legitimate listings sit in moderation for days and they leave the platform.

concepts.term: Image-reuse + NSFW signals from the media pipeline
concepts.definition: MediaScanner emits two fraud-relevant features per image: a reuse fingerprint (matches a global index of previously-seen images, weighted by whose listings they appeared on) and a content classifier score (NSFW, weapons, etc.). FraudSvc pulls these from MediaScanner (or from FeatureStore if pre-aggregated) and folds them into the risk score.
concepts.whyItMatters: Image-reuse is the highest-precision scam signal there is: a 'new' listing whose photos all appeared in a banned owner's listings last week is almost certainly the banned owner under a new account. Without media signals, fraud is missing its best feature.

whyNow: Bad listings are the worst possible search result — fraud must run before the search index sees the listing.

options.name: Conservative hold + thorough review (default for jobs/real-estate)
pros: Minimizes published scams; high trust for seekers.
Auditable per-listing decision.
cons: Slower time-to-publish for legit listings; higher moderator cost.
Owners complain about review latency.

view.caption: Borderline + above-threshold listings hold for human review before publication; trusted publishes go through; rejections tombstone the SearchDoc.

options.name: Permissive publish + retroactive takedown (default for low-risk marketplace)
pros: Listings appear instantly; low friction for owners.
Seeker reports + post-hoc ML scans catch most abuse.
cons: Some scams visible to seekers; reputation damage if visible too long.
Demands fast takedown infrastructure (delete propagation, cache invalidation).

view.caption: Listings publish immediately; post-hoc ML scans + seeker reports drive retroactive takedowns through the same indexer path.

failureDrills.scenario: ML fraud service is down for 30 minutes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rules-only mode kicks in: low-risk owners publish, anything that triggers a rule holds. Backlog drains when ML returns.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hard fallback policy documented; circuit breaker on ML calls; ops alerted on degraded-mode entry.

failureDrills.scenario: A new banned-phrase rule incorrectly holds 5% of legitimate listings.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Trust-and-safety hot-reverts the rule pack; held listings are auto-released without re-running the full pipeline.
failureDrills.mitigation: Rule packs versioned and hot-deployable; auto-release tooling rescans held listings against the new active rule set.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Alert fires; T&S can raise the auto-publish threshold for known-good owners or surge moderators.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-cohort backlog dashboards; owner-reputation-tiered queue prioritization.

failureDrills.scenario: A previously-banned owner creates a new account and reposts the same listings with rewritten descriptions but the SAME PHOTOS.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: MediaScanner's reuse-fingerprint matches the prior listings; FraudSvc adds the matched-banned-owner feature; the new listing is auto-rejected or held with a high-confidence explanation pointing at the prior banned account.
failureDrills.mitigation: Image-reuse fingerprint index is global (not per-owner); features include 'images previously appeared on banned/suspended owner'; moderator UI surfaces the matched prior listings so the decision is explainable.

strong: Combines hot-reloadable rules with ML scoring, with rules running first as a cheap filter.
Logs decision + triggered rules + model version for every listing.
Treats moderator decisions as lifecycle events (state transition + outbox) — not a separate pipeline.
Has an explicit degradation policy when ML is unavailable.
Closes the loop: moderator decisions feed training data + owner reputation; tracks moderation SLA.
weak: Pure ML with no explainability or rule layer.
Synchronous fraud scoring on the API write path.
Hard-fails publication when fraud service is down (or — worse — silently publishes everything).
Moderator approval bypasses the outbox / indexer and updates the search index directly.

traps.trap: Tuning the threshold without measuring false positives
traps.why: Cranking the threshold catches more fraud — and hides 30% of legitimate listings owners stop paying for.
traps.instead: Track FP/FN rates per owner cohort; report them to T&S as part of every rule change.

traps.trap: Allowing promoted listings to bypass fraud holds
traps.why: If a listing in fraud_review keeps its purchased boost, the platform is actively amplifying a likely scam.
traps.instead: FraudSvc writes promotion.state=blocked on hold; refund is part of the moderation runbook; fraud always wins over a paid promotion.

recap.after: Every listing is scored; bad ones go to humans with explanations; moderator decisions are lifecycle events with SLAs. (Forward-ref: in step 8, fraud state also blocks any paid promotion on the listing — no paid amplification of held content.)
recap.newRisk: Approved listings still need to reach the search index quickly, with deletes propagating correctly when listings are rejected, paused, or removed.

steps.title: 7. Search Indexing: Project the Listing DB into SearchDocs
description: Listing DB is the source of truth; the search index is a derived projection of `search_documents`. A transactional outbox writes one event per state change in the same transaction as the listing update, and a tail process publishes those events (with `sequence`) to the bus. The indexer consumes from the bus and upserts SearchDocs idempotently by listing_id, conditional on version — stale events (with `event.version <= doc.version`) are dropped, not applied.

Lifecycle drives indexing. A listing in state `published` is indexed; `paused`/`expired`/`rejected`/`deleted` triggers a tombstone delete (remove from the index + invalidate caches). When a moderator rejects, a `listing.rejected` event flows through the same path — no parallel takedown code.

Reindexing is a first-class operation: spin up a new index with the new schema, replay all currently-published listings into it (from the DB, not the bus), then alias-swap reads onto the new index. Old index stays around for instant rollback.
The indexer is partitioned by listing_id so per-listing order is preserved; index writes are batched + flushed on a small timer to keep throughput high without spiking commit lag.

view.caption: Listing DB is the truth; Outbox + CDC guarantees nothing is lost; the indexer projects DB state into a sharded search index with version-conditional upsert and explicit tombstones for deletes/unpublishes.

steps.decisionPrompt: A `listing.updated` event with version=8 arrives at the indexer after version=10 has already been applied. What does the indexer do — and how does the SearchDoc reflect a listing that is held by a moderator?
patterns: Source-of-truth + projected search index with tombstones

concepts.definition: Write the entity + a row to an outbox table in the same DB transaction; a tail process publishes outbox rows to the bus, exactly mirroring committed state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, you either dual-write (lose events on crash between writes) or rely on the indexer to scan the DB (slow + bursty).

concepts.definition: The indexer applies an upsert only if `event.version > doc.version` (ES `if_seq_no` / version semantics). Deletes are explicit tombstones that also bump version.
concepts.whyItMatters: Idempotent upsert alone isn't enough — stale events can revert state. Versioning makes the projection deterministic regardless of bus ordering.

concepts.definition: Build a new index version in the background, then atomically swap the read alias from the old to the new index.
concepts.whyItMatters: Schema changes and full reindexes happen without read downtime; rollback is one alias swap.

concepts.definition: Time from listing.published in DB to visible in search index — typically a few seconds at p50, ~minutes at p99.
concepts.whyItMatters: Owners and seekers feel lag concretely ('I published, why isn't it in search?'); SLO drives alerting and capacity planning.

concepts.definition: On `listing.updated`/`.deleted`, the indexer purges affected URLs from `QueryCache` and tags CDN entries for stale-while-revalidate refresh.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without explicit invalidation, deleted listings keep returning from cache long after they're gone from the index.

whyNow: Once the pipeline produces approved listings, the read surface needs them visible — fast, but never stale-overwriting newer state.

deepDives.title: SearchDoc shape + ranking
