points: Indexed fields: `title` (analyzed), `description` (analyzed), `category`/`subcategory` (keyword facets), `location` (geo_point), `price_value` + `price_band` (range + facet), `domain_fields` (per-category object: jobs.salary_min, real_estate.bedrooms, vehicle.year), `published_at`, `quality_score`, `owner_reputation`, `duplicate_group_id`, `fraud_status`.
Filter constraints: `fraud_status=clean`; only canonical of `duplicate_group_id` is indexed; `expires_at > now`.
Ranking: lexical relevance (BM25) × freshness decay × quality_score × owner_reputation × geo proximity (where applicable). Domain-specific boosts per category.
Pagination: cursor / `search_after` (sort by [score desc, listing_id]) — not page-number offsets, which become unstable under live index changes.
Cache invalidation rules: short TTL (60s-5min) on search results; long TTL (1h+) with stale-while-revalidate on listing detail JSON; explicit purge on lifecycle events.

Promotion: an optional `promotion_boost` multiplier (capped, e.g. ≤1.5x) and a `promotion_active` filter let paid boost ride the same formula — bounded so a clearly-relevant unpromoted listing still wins (see step 8).

deepDives.title: Lifecycle → SearchDoc state
points: published → upsert SearchDoc (if version > current).
updated → upsert SearchDoc (if version > current); invalidate caches for affected URLs.
paused / expired → tombstone (delete from index) + invalidate caches.
rejected (fraud) → tombstone.

deleted → tombstone (delete doc from SearchIdx) + purge media (originals + thumbnails from MediaStore) + invalidate caches + erase PII; keep listing row with tombstoned_at (GDPR-compliant audit). The indexer drives all of this off the same listing.deleted event so projections stay in lockstep.
duplicate detected → only canonical indexed; others omitted; canonical change triggers upsert + tombstone.

deepDives.title: Blue/green reindex
points: Build new index alongside old; replay all `state=published` listings from the DB (not the bus).
Validate doc count + sample queries against old index.
Atomic alias swap; old index retained for 24h for rollback.
Live writes continue against the bus during the rebuild; once swapped, the new index catches up on the bus tail.

flows.name: Outbox-driven indexing with version protection
flows.note: One transaction guarantees no published listing is missing from the bus; version-conditional upsert handles out-of-order bus delivery and replays.

messages.label: INSERT listing v=N + outbox row (same tx)

options.name: Outbox + CDC indexer (default)
pros: Cannot lose events even on producer crash.
Decouples write commit latency from index latency.
Works the same for any downstream projection (analytics, ML feature store, audit).
cons: Outbox table grows; needs a trim job.
Tail process is one more thing to operate.

view.caption: Listing writes + outbox row in one DB transaction; tail process publishes to the bus; indexer projects into SearchIdx.

options.name: Dual-write: API writes DB + search synchronously
pros: No tail process, no outbox table.
cons: If the search write fails or the API crashes between, the index drifts from the DB.
Couples API latency to search-cluster latency.

view.caption: API writes both stores directly — no tail process, but the index drifts whenever one write fails after the other succeeds.

failureDrills.scenario: Indexer falls behind by 30 minutes during a 10M-row import.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads still serve the (slightly stale) old documents; backlog drains as the indexer catches up; alerting fires on lag SLO breach.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition indexer autoscaling on bus lag; index writes batched; alerts on index_lag > SLO.

failureDrills.scenario: Bad schema change ships and starts breaking documents in the live index.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Alias-swap reverts to the previous index version in seconds; new index is rebuilt cleanly on a fixed schema.
failureDrills.mitigation: Blue/green deploys mandatory for schema changes; never write a breaking change to the live alias.

failureDrills.scenario: An old `listing.updated` v=8 event arrives after v=10 was applied (consumer restart, replay).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Indexer skips it (version condition fails); doc stays at v=10.
failureDrills.mitigation: Version-conditional upsert; events carry monotonic version per listing_id.

strong: Names the source of truth explicitly and treats the index as a derived projection.
Uses transactional outbox (or CDC) — not dual writes.
Version-conditional upsert + explicit tombstones for deletes.
Has blue/green reindexing for schema changes.
Connects lifecycle state to SearchDoc presence + cache invalidation.
weak: Treats the search index as the source of truth.
Dual-writes from the API to DB + search and 'hopes both succeed'.
Idempotent upsert with no version check (stale events overwrite fresh state).

traps.trap: Reindexing by 'updating the live index in place'
traps.why: A schema bug breaks all reads, and you can't roll back without re-ingesting.
traps.instead: Always build a new index alongside; alias-swap to switch; keep the old index for fast rollback.

traps.trap: Idempotent upsert without version check
traps.why: A replayed old event silently overwrites the current document.
traps.instead: Use the index's optimistic concurrency primitive (ES `if_seq_no` / version) gated on event.version.

recap.before: Approved listings only existed in the DB.
recap.after: Approved listings flow into search with bounded lag, ordered by version, with explicit tombstones for deletes — reindexing is routine.
recap.newRisk: Search now reflects truth — but the ranker is purely organic. Owners will (rightly) ask whether they can pay to show up higher; that monetization layer is next, and it has to fit inside the ranker without breaking seeker trust.

steps.title: 8. Promoted Listings: Boost Without Breaking Relevance
description: Owners can buy promotion products (feature a single listing, sponsor a category, top-of-category, daily-budget sponsored search). A `PromotionSvc` reads `listing_promotions` and the indexer projects an active promotion's `promotion_boost` onto the `SearchDoc` at upsert time — so the ranker can blend it with relevance / freshness / quality / owner reputation without a separate code path.

The hard rule: boost is one capped term in the ranking formula, not a master switch. A boosted listing that's badly off-query (wrong category, wrong city, wrong price band) must lose to a relevant unpromoted listing. A maximum `boost_factor` (e.g. 1.5x), explicit ranking-floor filters (must clear minimum relevance score for the query), and a hard cap on the share of promoted slots per page (e.g. at most N of the top 10) keep the seeker experience honest.

Promoted results are labeled in the UI ('Sponsored', 'Featured') — a legal requirement in many jurisdictions and a trust requirement everywhere. The BFF stamps the label from `search_documents.promotion_label`; the CDN is allowed to cache labeled results.

Billing + pacing run async. The BFF logs every impression/click to `Analytics`; `Analytics` feeds `BillingSvc`, which charges the owner, decrements `budget_remaining`, and emits `promotion.exhausted` when the budget is gone — the indexer then upserts the SearchDoc with `promotion_active=false`. Pacing decides whether to spend a daily budget ASAP or smooth it across the day.

Fraud has the last word: a listing in `fraud_review` or `rejected` state has its promotion blocked, regardless of whether the owner paid — `FraudSvc` flips the promotion to `blocked` in `PromotionDB` on hold. You do not sell paid amplification to scams.

view.caption: Owner buys promotion → BillingSvc writes listing_promotions → outbox event → indexer projects boost onto SearchDoc. Impressions/clicks flow through Analytics back to BillingSvc, which exhausts/expires the promotion. FraudSvc can block at any point.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you let an owner pay to rank higher without seekers losing trust — and what stops a scammer from buying their way to the top?
patterns: Source-of-truth + projected search index with tombstones

concepts.term: Promotion as a capped ranking signal
concepts.definition: promotion_boost is one multiplicative term in the ranking formula, bounded (e.g. ≤1.5x) and gated by a minimum relevance floor for the query. A boosted listing that fails the floor never appears.
concepts.whyItMatters: Uncapped boost = pay to spam. Capped boost = pay for visibility among relevant results. Only the second is sustainable.

concepts.definition: At most N promoted slots per result page (e.g. 2 of the top 10) and never the only result for a query.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, a category with many active boosts can become 100% paid — seekers see no organic results and leave.

concepts.definition: Promoted results carry a UI label ('Sponsored', 'Featured') stamped from search_documents.promotion_label.
concepts.whyItMatters: FTC + EU DSA require it; trust requires it. Hidden ads are the fastest way to a regulatory action.

concepts.definition: Two modes: spend the daily budget as fast as auctions allow (asap), or smooth it across hours (even_throughout_day). When budget_remaining hits zero, promotion deactivates within seconds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without pacing, big budgets win every auction in the first hour and an owner's daily campaign disappears by noon.

concepts.definition: BFF writes events to Analytics; BillingSvc consumes them; the read path never blocks on a synchronous billing call.
concepts.whyItMatters: Synchronous billing in the read path is a latency and availability liability — the search result must not depend on the billing service being up.

concepts.definition: A listing in fraud_review or rejected has its promotions flipped to blocked; refunds queued; the indexer un-boosts the SearchDoc.
concepts.whyItMatters: If a scam can be boosted, the platform is monetizing harm. The fraud loop must explicitly include promotion state.

whyNow: Search-indexing just landed; promotion plugs into the same SearchDoc + ranker + outbox machinery as one capped extra ranking signal. Doing it BEFORE the read path is designed means the seeker-experience step can treat 'Sponsored' labeling as a baked-in input, and the scale step can include promotion budgets in its multi-tenant fairness story.

options.name: Capped multiplicative boost in the ranking formula (default)
pros: Reuses the existing ranker; one extra field on the SearchDoc.
Boost is one term among many; relevance still dominates for clearly-relevant unpromoted results.
Easy to tune the cap globally to balance revenue vs seeker trust.
cons: Owners with deep pockets always win the boost auction at the cap.
Hard for owners to predict slot position from a boost number alone.

view.caption: PromotionSvc projects an active boost onto the SearchDoc at index time; the ranker treats it like any other signal, capped so relevance still dominates.

options.name: Guaranteed top slot for a fixed fee
pros: Predictable for owners; easy to price.
Simple ranker change: 'pin top slot for active feature, else organic'.
cons: Top slot can be wildly off-query — destroys seeker trust fastest of any model.
Hard to balance multiple competing 'featured' owners for the same slot.

view.caption: PromotionSvc returns a pinned listing; BFF prepends it ahead of organic results — simple, but the pinned slot can be wildly off-query.

options.name: Real-time auction at query time
pros: Maximizes platform revenue per query (the bid that wins is the highest-value bid).
Tightly tied to demand; price floats with competition.
cons: Adds an auction service in the read path — latency + availability risk.
Complex to operate and explain; hard to keep cheap on a 50M-search/day surface.

view.caption: PromotionSvc runs an auction against BillingSvc on every query — maximises revenue but adds a synchronous service to the read path.

flows.name: Buy promotion → SearchDoc gets boost (labeled)
flows.note: Same outbox + indexer machinery as any other lifecycle event. Boost is a SearchDoc field; the ranker reads it like any other ranking signal.

flows.name: Seeker query → impression billing → budget exhaustion
flows.note: Read path never blocks on billing. Exhaustion takes seconds to propagate (event → indexer → upsert). Acceptable lag: a small over-spend within one indexer batch.

messages.label: query (boost in formula, slot-share cap, relevance floor)

failureDrills.scenario: BillingSvc is down for 30 minutes mid-day.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Seeker reads continue serving boosted results (state in SearchDoc is fine); impression logs queue in Analytics; on recovery, BillingSvc catches up — some owners may slightly overspend their daily cap.
failureDrills.mitigation: Async billing decoupled from read path; bounded backlog with alerting; refund / credit policy for documented overspend.

failureDrills.scenario: An owner buys promotion for a listing that later goes into fraud_review.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: FraudSvc flips the promotion to blocked; indexer un-boosts the SearchDoc; refund queued.
failureDrills.mitigation: FraudSvc writes to PromotionDB on hold; refund is part of the moderation runbook; never silently keep charging.

failureDrills.scenario: A new ranking experiment boosts promoted listings too aggressively; seeker CTR on organic drops.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A/B detects the regression; rollback to the previous boost cap; no permanent damage because the cap is config, not code.
failureDrills.mitigation: boost_factor cap and slot-share cap live in config; ranking experiments report seeker-experience metrics (organic CTR, time-to-first-relevant-click) alongside revenue.

strong: Treats boost as ONE capped ranking term, not a separate code path or master switch.
Enforces a relevance floor + slot-share cap so boost can't dominate.
Discloses promoted results in the UI (legal + trust).
Billing + pacing run async; never on the read path.
Fraud explicitly blocks promotion — no paid amplification of scams.
Names the relevance-vs-revenue tradeoff and how the system measures both (organic CTR, time-to-first-relevant-click, revenue).
weak: Pinning the paid result to the top regardless of relevance.
Synchronous billing call in the search request path.
Promoted results indistinguishable from organic in the UI.
No interaction between fraud_review and active promotions.

traps.trap: Treating promotion as a master switch that overrides ranking
traps.why: A boosted irrelevant listing at the top destroys seeker trust faster than any other quality bug.
traps.instead: Promotion is one capped multiplicative term in the ranker, gated by a minimum relevance floor for the query.

traps.trap: No slot-share cap per page
traps.why: Categories with many active boosts become 100% paid; seekers stop trusting search.
traps.instead: Cap promoted slots per result page (e.g. ≤2 of top 10); never make a query's only result a promoted one.

traps.trap: Synchronous billing on the read path
traps.why: Couples seeker search availability to billing-service availability; one outage takes search down.
traps.instead: Read path logs impressions to Analytics asynchronously; BillingSvc consumes downstream.

traps.trap: Not blocking promotion when a listing is held for fraud
traps.why: You end up monetizing fraud — actively amplifying a scam the platform already flagged.
traps.instead: FraudSvc writes promotion.state=blocked on hold; indexer un-boosts; refund is part of the moderation runbook.

recap.before: Search ranks organically by relevance / freshness / quality / owner reputation.
recap.after: Owners can pay for a capped boost as one ranking term; promoted results are labeled and capped per page; billing + pacing run async; fraud always wins.
recap.newRisk: Revenue and seeker trust are now linked metrics — both must be measured (organic CTR, time-to-first-relevant-click, revenue per query) and changes to the boost cap need explicit A/B. The next step (seeker read path) has to surface the 'Sponsored' label clearly and never cache personalized promoted results at the CDN.

steps.title: 9. Seeker Read Path: CDN-Cached Search, BFF-Composed Detail
description: The public read surface is fronted by a CDN. Search responses are CDN-cached by URL (with vary on query + locale) for short TTLs; listing detail pages and JSON are cached aggressively because they change rarely after publication.

Media is served from its own CDN on a separate hostname (`img.example.com`) directly off `MediaStore` — cookie-less, immutably cached, and on its own failure domain. See the Separate media origin concept below.

Behind the CDN, a Backend-for-Frontend composes the page: it queries the search index for results, fetches detail rows from the listing DB on cache miss, and stitches in personalization (saved searches, recently viewed, saved listings) from session state. A query cache in front of the search index absorbs repeated queries (the long tail of 'jobs in berlin' that fires thousands of times).

Seeker-facing features — saved listings, saved searches with alerts, report-listing, recently viewed — all live on the read surface: write through the Public API into their own tables; the `AlertWorker` consumes new/updated listings, matches them against `saved_searches`, and enqueues notifications via `Notif`. Owner analytics is a separate projection from read events captured by `Analytics`, never on the request path.

Critically, the read path has no synchronous dependency on the write pipeline: if the indexer is hours behind, seekers still get the last-known good search results, just slightly stale. This is the non-negotiable: ingestion can degrade; seeker search cannot. 'Always available' here means stale-but-served, not perfect availability under a full regional outage.
Promoted-result labeling is baked into the SearchDoc, so labeled non-personalized responses are still fully CDN-cacheable. Per-user personalization and ad targeting stay in the BFF, never on the CDN.

view.caption: CDN absorbs most search/detail traffic; BFF composes search + detail; QueryCache absorbs the long tail. Media is served via a SEPARATE Media CDN on its own cookie-less origin (immutable long-TTL cache). Saved-search alerts ride the same event bus as the indexer. Read path has zero synchronous dependency on the write pipeline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the worst thing that can happen on the seeker read path if the ingestion pipeline is completely down? And how does a seeker get an email when a new listing matches their saved search?

concepts.definition: The seeker read path's only dependency on the write pipeline is data freshness — never request-path coupling. 'Always available' means stale results, not perfect availability under regional outage.
concepts.whyItMatters: Ingestion is bursty, AI/geocoding/fraud have spiky latencies, and any of them can fail. Reads serving 50M searches/day cannot wait for writes.

concepts.definition: A per-surface (web, mobile) service that composes results from search index + DB + personalization in one round trip, returning exactly what the surface needs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Mobile is bandwidth- and latency-constrained; web wants SEO-friendly server-rendered HTML. Separate BFFs let each shape its responses without bloating the others.

concepts.definition: CDN caches at the edge by URL (close to seeker, but per-region). Query cache sits in front of the search index (one per region, shared across BFFs).
concepts.whyItMatters: CDN handles the most popular queries; query cache handles the long tail that varies per session but repeats often enough to matter.

concepts.definition: The `AlertWorker` subscribes to `listing.published` / `listing.updated` events and matches each against active `saved_searches`. Matches enqueue `Notif` (email/push) honoring per-subscription throttle.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without this, 'alerts' is just a UI checkbox with no backend. With it, alert quality drives seeker retention.

concepts.definition: Serve cached content immediately, refresh asynchronously; new cached value is used for the next request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Even with a 5-minute index lag SLO, seekers don't see the lag — they see fast, slightly older results.

concepts.definition: Search results carry a `promotion_label` ('Sponsored', 'Featured') the BFF surfaces in the UI; the CDN is allowed to cache labeled responses because the label is part of the indexed SearchDoc.
concepts.whyItMatters: Disclosure is a legal requirement in many jurisdictions (FTC, EU DSA) and a trust requirement everywhere. See step 8 for the ranking blend that keeps promoted results from dominating.

concepts.definition: Media (originals + thumbnails) is served from its own CDN on a distinct hostname pointing at `MediaStore`, cookie-less and with immutable long-TTL caching (content-hashed keys). The API origin and the media origin share nothing operationally.
concepts.whyItMatters: Same-origin serving forces one cache policy on two very different traffic shapes: API responses (short TTL, auth cookies) vs media (immutable, anonymous). Splitting them lets each be sized, cached, and operated correctly — and a media-CDN incident no longer takes down search.

whyNow: Now that there's data to serve, the read path's availability AND the seeker product surface (saved/alerts/reports) become the headline SLO.

options.name: CDN + BFF + query cache (default)
pros: Three layers of caching; most traffic never reaches the search cluster.
BFF lets web and mobile evolve independently.
Index outage = stale results, not 5xx.
cons: More moving parts to monitor (CDN, BFF, query cache, index).
Cache invalidation requires care on listing updates.

view.caption: Edge cache absorbs most search/detail; BFF composes the page; query cache absorbs repeated long-tail queries; index outage degrades to stale-but-served.

options.name: Public API hits the search index directly (no CDN, no BFF)
pros: Simplest topology.
cons: Every search request hits the cluster; cluster capacity dictates seeker capacity.
No buffer when the indexer or cluster degrades.

view.caption: Every search request hits the cluster — simplest topology, but cluster capacity now dictates seeker capacity and an outage is a 5xx, not stale.

failureDrills.scenario: Search index cluster experiences a 10-minute partial outage.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: CDN and query cache continue serving popular queries; only long-tail, uncached queries error.
failureDrills.mitigation: Long CDN TTLs with stale-while-revalidate; BFF returns 'try again' for cache misses, not 5xx; index has multi-AZ replicas.

failureDrills.scenario: Ingestion pipeline halts entirely for 2 hours after an outbox bug.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Seekers see the listings as of 2 hours ago; no errors; alerts fire on index_lag breach.
failureDrills.mitigation: Pipeline replay from outbox once fixed; no read-path code path depends on ingestion liveness.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Report row created; listing routed into ModQueue with priority bump; visible in mod tools; seeker gets confirmation.
failureDrills.mitigation: Abuse-report API with per-seeker rate limit; multiple reports raise priority but never auto-takedown.

strong: Names the read/write isolation explicitly: ingestion can degrade, search cannot.
Uses both CDN and query cache (different layers, different hits).
Backs saved-listings / saved-searches / alerts / report-listing with real tables, APIs, and workers.
Stale-while-revalidate / soft-fail behavior on cache miss + downstream issues.
Distinguishes 'stale-but-served' from 'perfectly available under regional outage'.
weak: BFF or API blocks on synchronous writes / indexing.
One global cache TTL, ignoring that detail pages are far more cacheable than search results.
'Saved searches and alerts' is mentioned but never backed by storage or a worker.
No plan for what reads do when the index is down.

traps.trap: Caching personalized results at the CDN
traps.why: Edge cache returns one seeker's saved listings to another seeker.
traps.instead: Cache non-personalized search/detail at the CDN; do personalization in the BFF where session is in scope.

traps.trap: Saved-search alerts as a UI promise with no backend
traps.why: Owners think alerts are 'free' but every match must be evaluated; without a worker + throttle, the platform either silently never fires or floods users.
traps.instead: AlertWorker consumes the same listing.* bus, matches saved queries, and honors per-subscription throttle (instant / daily / weekly).

traps.trap: Serving images from the API origin (same hostname as /api)
traps.why: API responses carry auth cookies and have short TTLs; bundling images onto the same origin forces every image request to carry those cookies too (larger requests, less cacheable at the edge), and ties media's failure domain to the API. A media-CDN incident shouldn't take search down — and vice versa.
traps.instead: Serve media from its own cookie-less hostname (e.g. img.example.com) fronted by a Media CDN with origin pull from MediaStore. Use content-hashed object keys so cache-control: immutable + year-long TTLs are safe.

recap.before: Listings indexed but no scalable read path; product features were UI-only promises.
recap.after: 50M searches/day absorbed by CDN + query cache; index outages are stale-not-5xx; saved/alerts/reports are real systems.
recap.newRisk: All this works at average load; tail-latency events and noisy tenants in the write pipeline still need handling.

steps.title: 10. Scale, Multi-Tenancy, and Reliability
description: At 50k owners, ingestion is multi-tenant. Partition the event bus by owner_id (composite with listing_id where ordering matters) so one partner's 10M-row drop doesn't head-of-line-block everyone else's writes. A fair scheduler weights worker capacity across owners; per-owner rate limits in the API enforce backpressure at the entry point.

Hot partitions still happen when one owner dominates an order of magnitude. Mitigation: sub-partition the hot owner (compose key with `listing_id` hash so they fan across N partitions), dedicate worker capacity to drain the hot lane, or temporarily throttle to protect platform SLOs while ops responds.

Pipeline stages each scale on their own lag SLO. The DLQ has an operating loop: alert → inspect → fix root cause → replay. Reindexing, replay, and recovery are first-class workflows — runbook-driven, not heroics. Event schema evolution uses versioned events + dual-write windows so replays of old events through new consumers still apply correctly.

Disaster recovery: the source of truth (listing DB) replicates cross-region (RPO ≈ seconds, RTO ≈ minutes for active-passive failover). The search index can be rebuilt from the DB anywhere (RTO ≈ hours but reads stay stale-served on the surviving region). The raw-file store is the ultimate replay source for ingestion failures.

view.caption: Per-owner partitioning + fair scheduling + per-stage autoscaling + first-class DLQ operations + versioned events for replay-safety. The whole system fails partially, not totally.

steps.decisionPrompt: One partner pushes a corrupt 10M-row file at the same time another partner does their normal weekly drop. How do you keep both moving — and how do you handle the partner who is 10x bigger than everyone else?
patterns: Webhook delivery as a distributed system

concepts.definition: Bus partitioning key includes owner_id so one tenant's traffic occupies a bounded subset of partitions.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, a 10M-row drop monopolizes consumer threads and starves every other owner.

concepts.definition: When one owner dominates a partition: sub-partition by (owner_id, listing_id_hash); dedicate workers to the hot lane; or throttle to protect platform SLOs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Per-owner partitioning helps until one owner is bigger than the rest combined — then the same problem moves down one layer.

concepts.definition: Consumer assigns capacity weighted across owners (and possibly priorities), not strictly partition-round-robin.
concepts.whyItMatters: Static partition assignment can still let a hot owner saturate the workers handling their partitions; the scheduler reallocates.

concepts.definition: Alert on DLQ arrivals → triage → fix root cause → replay (or discard with audit). A DLQ is a worklist, not a graveyard.
concepts.whyItMatters: Most production data loss happens because DLQs filled and got 'cleaned out' instead of replayed.

concepts.definition: Each event has `event_version`; consumers handle multiple versions; replays use the consumer that matches the event's version (or a forward-compatible reader).
concepts.whyItMatters: Replaying year-old events through today's consumer is how silent data corruption happens; versioning makes it explicit.

concepts.definition: RPO (data-loss budget) ≈ seconds for the listing DB (sync replication). RTO (downtime budget) ≈ minutes for the listing DB (DNS / VIP failover); ≈ hours for full search index rebuild. Reads stay stale-served on the surviving region.
concepts.whyItMatters: Interview-quality DR isn't 'cross-region replication' — it's named numbers and a story for who's serving reads during the gap.

concepts.definition: Tooling that re-emits a cohort (date range, owner, model version) into a pipeline stage, idempotent against current state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bad enrichment, bad fraud rules, schema changes — all need fast, safe reprocessing without disturbing live writes.

whyNow: The architecture works for the happy path; production is what happens when one component goes wrong AND another is slow.

deepDives.title: Backpressure all the way through
points: API rate-limits per owner (token bucket in RateLimitStore) — partners get 429 instead of taking down the bus.
Bus partitions per owner — one owner's lag is one owner's lag, not the platform's.
Stages autoscale on per-partition lag, not aggregate lag.
Indexer batches and applies a write-rate ceiling to protect the search cluster.

deepDives.title: Disaster recovery + recovery from bad code
points: Listing DB: synchronous cross-AZ replication; async cross-region with a documented RPO (seconds) and RTO (minutes for active-passive).
Search index: fully rebuildable from listing DB in the surviving region; reads stay stale-served on the original side during the gap.
Raw file store: cross-region replicated; the ultimate replay source for ingestion failures.
Every pipeline stage has a 'replay from X' tool, idempotent against current state.
Bad model / bad rules: revert + reprocess affected cohort; never restore from backup if the truth row is intact.

deepDives.title: Observability that maps to SLOs
points: Per-stage lag dashboards (consumer offset vs latest).
Per-owner API usage, error rate, and DLQ rate.
Search index freshness (publish_to_visible histogram) and query latency.
Fraud decision dashboards: FP/FN rates per cohort, model version, rule pack.
Moderation backlog + SLA per cohort; webhook delivery success / DLQ depth per owner.

deepDives.title: Promotion & billing ops metrics
points: Overspend rate: fraction of promotions that exceed daily budget by more than tolerance (target <0.1%; non-zero is expected because billing is async).
Billing backlog: lag between impression/click events and BillingSvc consumption (alert when > a few minutes).
Promotion exhaustion lag: time from budget=0 to SearchDoc upserted with promotion_active=false (target seconds, bounded by indexer batch interval).
Organic CTR regression: A/B-detected drop in click-through on UNpromoted results after a boost-cap or slot-share-cap change — gates the change before full rollout.
Paid-result quality: time-to-first-relevant-click and bounce rate on promoted slots (the seeker-trust early warning).

strong: Partitions the bus by tenant explicitly, with fair scheduling on top of partitioning AND hot-partition mitigation.
Treats DLQ as a worklist with an operating loop, not a dead-end.
Names the recovery story (reprocess, reindex, replay) as a first-class workflow with versioned events.
Maps observability to SLOs the platform commits to (search freshness, index lag, moderation SLA, webhook success).
Cites concrete RPO/RTO numbers and what serves reads during a regional gap.
weak: Assumes scaling is just 'add workers'.
Has no per-tenant isolation; one bad partner can take down ingestion.
DLQ alerts but nobody owns the loop to actually process them.
Replays old events through new consumers without versioning, hoping nothing breaks.

traps.trap: Skipping per-tenant partitioning because 'we're not that big yet'
traps.why: The first 10M-row file from a single tenant takes down the whole pipeline; refactoring under pressure is far worse than designing it in from the start.
traps.instead: Make tenant partitioning the default; even at low scale it costs almost nothing.

recap.before: Single-tenant happy path, no recovery story.
recap.after: Per-tenant isolation + hot-partition mitigation, autoscaled stages, DLQ + replay as ops workflows, versioned events, search reads always-on, named RPO/RTO.
recap.newRisk: More partitions and lanes to operate; routing keys, hot-partition rebalancing, and per-stage SLOs become explicit owners' responsibilities.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Listing Platform
finalDesign.description: External systems push listings via authenticated APIs (accept-fast → versioned events on bus) or drop files into per-owner SFTP directories (file watcher → streaming parser → bus); owners and admins use the same APIs through an admin panel with AI-assisted authoring (suggestions logged, never auto-published). The shared event bus is partitioned per owner. A pipeline of independently-scaling stages — normalize, enrich (geocode/classify/translate/score), media (fetch/scan/strip/transcode), dedup (exact then fuzzy with domain-aware blocking + ANN), and fraud (rules + ML + human review whose decisions are themselves lifecycle events) — writes the versioned listing DB. A transactional outbox + indexer projects listings into a sharded search index with version-conditional upsert and explicit tombstone deletes; blue/green reindex handles schema evolution. Seekers hit a CDN-cached BFF that reads from the search index and listing DB. Saved-search alerts ride the same bus as the indexer. The read path has zero synchronous dependency on the write pipeline; webhooks deliver owner callbacks with signing + retry + DLQ; every state change and decision is auditable. An optional promotion layer (BillingSvc + PromotionDB + PromotionSvc) lets owners buy a capped boost that the indexer projects onto the SearchDoc as one ranking term — labeled in the UI, paced and billed async, blocked on fraud hold. Media (originals + thumbnails) is served from a separate Media CDN on its own cookie-less hostname, fronting MediaStore directly — decoupled from the API origin's cache policy, auth, and failure domain.

view.caption: All ingress (FTP, API, manual + AI) converges on a partitioned event bus carrying versioned lifecycle events. Independent pipeline stages (normalize, enrich, media, dedup, fraud) write the listing DB. Outbox + CDC drive the search indexer (version-conditional, blue/green). Saved-search alerts ride the same bus. Seekers hit a CDN-cached BFF over the search index and listing DB — fully decoupled from the write pipeline.

flows.name: Happy path: partner POST → seeker visible
flows.note: Async pipeline behind the bus; outbox guarantees the indexer never misses a publish; version-conditional upsert protects against stale events; reads have no synchronous dependency on writes.

flows.name: FTP import: file → row events → status callback
flows.note: Files land in object storage; rows fan into the same bus as the API; partner is notified asynchronously with per-row error report.

flows.name: Moderation: held listing → approve or reject
flows.note: Moderator decisions are lifecycle events on the same bus as the indexer — no parallel takedown code path.

flows.name: Delete / GDPR erasure: tombstone all projections
flows.note: Listing row stays for audit (tombstoned_at + erased PII); index doc, caches, and media are removed. Version+1 protects against late stale updates.

flows.name: Seeker read path: CDN → query cache → search index → DB
flows.note: Three caching layers absorb most traffic; an index outage degrades to stale-but-served, never to 5xx.

flows.name: Webhook delivery: sign, retry, DLQ, replay
flows.note: Owner endpoints fail; the dispatcher retries with backoff, logs every attempt, and DLQs permanent failures with a replay UI.

flows.name: Promotion lifecycle: buy → boost → bill → exhaust
flows.note: Boost is a SearchDoc field; billing runs async from impressions; exhaustion deactivates within seconds. Fraud can short-circuit the boost at any time.

messages.label: query (capped boost + slot-share cap + relevance floor)

functional.requirement: FTP imports for bulk listings (CSV/XML/JSON, possibly compressed) with full/incremental semantics
functional.how: Per-owner SFTP → object-storage landing zone (with file-hash dedup) → streaming parser → row events on the bus. import_mode (full vs incremental) drives expiration semantics.

functional.requirement: API for create / update / partial-update / delete / bulk
functional.how: Owner API authenticates, rate-limits per owner, idempotently publishes versioned events; bulk returns a job id with status + signed webhook.

functional.requirement: Lifecycle: state machine with version ordering + tombstones
functional.how: Every event carries (sequence, event_version); listing_state_transitions logs every move; deletes propagate as explicit tombstones to SearchIdx + caches + MediaStore.

functional.requirement: Manual + AI-assisted listing authoring with guardrails
functional.how: Admin panel uses the Owner API; AISvc serves PII-redacted interactive suggestions; ai_authoring_events logs every suggestion; nothing auto-publishes.

functional.requirement: Listing enrichment (geocode, classify, translate, quality, completeness)
functional.how: Enrichment orchestrator runs independent enrichers in parallel; outputs are versioned and separable from owner-provided fields.
MediaWorker fetches owner URLs; MediaScanner virus-scans, strips EXIF, NSFW-classifies, fingerprints reuse; transcodes + thumbnails written to immutable MediaStore.

functional.requirement: Duplicate detection across owners and channels
functional.how: Exact dedup on (owner_id, external_id); fuzzy dedup with domain-aware blocking + ANN; borderline → moderator queue; canonical-of-group is indexed.

functional.requirement: Fraud prevention with rules + ML and human review
functional.how: Hot-reloadable rule packs + ML scoring with feature store; auto-publish low risk, hold-for-review borderline (24h SLA), auto-reject high risk; moderator decisions are lifecycle events.

functional.requirement: Near-real-time search index with ranking + tombstones
functional.how: Transactional outbox + CDC + version-conditional indexer; SearchDoc carries ranking signals; tombstones on delete/pause/expire/reject; blue/green reindexing.
CDN-cached BFF reads search index + listing DB; query cache absorbs long-tail searches; stale-while-revalidate; cursor pagination.

functional.requirement: Saved listings, saved searches with alerts
functional.how: saved_listings + saved_searches tables; AlertWorker consumes listing.published/updated and matches against saved searches; Notif delivers with per-subscription throttle.
POST /listings/{id}/reports writes listing_reports; routes to ModQueue with priority bump on multiple reports.

functional.requirement: Admin panel for owners and platform admins (RBAC)
functional.how: Admin UI hits the same APIs with owner-scoped or admin-scoped tokens; every action audited in hash-chained audit_log.

functional.requirement: Owner-facing import status + error reports + signed webhook callbacks
functional.how: import_jobs/import_records back the status API and error report CSV; Webhooks dispatcher signs callbacks (HMAC), retries with backoff, dead-letters with replay UI.
Analytics pipeline captures seeker events from the BFF (read path); derived projection only — never a request-path dependency.

functional.requirement: Promoted listings ('boost') as a paid product with relevance + trust guardrails
functional.how: BillingSvc + PromotionDB + PromotionSvc; capped boost is one ranking term in the SearchDoc; slot-share cap + relevance floor; 'Sponsored' labeling; async impression-based billing; FraudSvc blocks paid amplification of held listings.

nonFunctional.requirement: Seeker reads stale-but-served when ingestion is degraded
nonFunctional.how: Read path depends only on the search index + listing DB; no synchronous link to the write pipeline; CDN + query cache + SWR.
Outbox-driven indexing with per-partition autoscaling on bus lag; version-conditional upsert; index lag is an SLO.
Idempotency-Key dedupes producer retries; (owner_id, external_id) unique constraint dedupes logical listings across channels; event sequence prevents stale-update overwrite.
Per-owner partitioning on the bus, per-owner rate limits at the API (state in RateLimitStore), fair scheduling across consumer groups, sub-partitioning for dominant owners.

nonFunctional.requirement: Auditability of every state change, decision, and admin action
nonFunctional.how: listing_state_transitions + hash-chained audit_log; fraud_decisions, duplicate_candidates, ai_authoring_events capture inputs + outputs + versions; outbox preserves event stream.
DELETE flow propagates: ListingDB tombstoned_at + PII erased; SearchIdx tombstone; QueryCache + CDN invalidate; MediaStore purge; FeatureStore aged-out.

nonFunctional.requirement: Recoverability (bad import, bad model, bad rules)
nonFunctional.how: Raw-file store + outbox + replay tools per stage; versioned events + replay-safe consumers; blue/green search reindex; reprocessing is a first-class workflow.
Listing DB cross-region replication (RPO ≈ seconds, RTO ≈ minutes active-passive); search index rebuildable from DB (RTO ≈ hours); raw files cross-region for ingestion replay.

nonFunctional.requirement: Webhook delivery at-least-once with signing + DLQ
nonFunctional.how: webhook_deliveries logs every attempt; HMAC signed; exponential backoff; DLQ on permanent failure with owner-visible replay UI.

nonFunctional.requirement: Promoted listings respect seeker trust (disclosure, relevance floor, slot-share cap, fraud override, async billing)
nonFunctional.how: Boost is one capped multiplicative term in the ranker with a minimum relevance floor and a per-page slot-share cap; results carry a 'Sponsored' UI label (search_documents.promotion_label); FraudSvc writes promotion.state=blocked on hold (no paid amplification of fraud); BillingSvc consumes impression/click events asynchronously off the read path.

say: Confirm the dominant tension: high-throughput multi-tenant writes feeding a low-latency stale-but-served read surface.
Pin down scale: 10M active / 500M historical / 50k owners / 50M searches per day / 5-min freshness target.
Ask: how strict is read availability when ingestion is degraded? (Answer: it's the headline SLO — stale, not 5xx.)
Clarify lifecycle: create, update, delete, expire — and that GDPR erasure must propagate everywhere.
Walk through the synchronous create-and-index design.
Enumerate concretely what fails: 10M-row FTP files, slow geocoder, indexer outage, noisy partner, stale-event ordering.
Decouple: API as accept-fast, publish versioned events to a partitioned event bus.
Add FTP path that converges on the same row events; full-snapshot vs incremental semantics explicit.
Async pipeline: normalize → enrich (parallel) → media safety → dedup (exact then fuzzy with domain blocking) → fraud (rules + ML + human as lifecycle events).
Source-of-truth versioned listing DB + outbox + version-conditional indexer; blue/green reindex.
SearchDoc shape + ranking + tombstone delete + cache invalidation.
CDN-cached BFF for seeker reads; saved searches / alerts / reports / analytics as real systems.

OPTIONAL / time-permitting: promoted listings (step 8) is an advanced extension — the capped-boost + relevance-floor + labeling + fraud-blocking + async-billing story. Skip in a tight session; raise it as a discussion if there's time.
Per-tenant partitioning + fair scheduling + hot-partition mitigation.
DLQ operating loop and replay tooling; versioned events for replay-safety.
Disaster recovery: DB cross-region with named RPO/RTO, index rebuildable, raw files preserved.
Webhook delivery as its own distributed system.
Trade-offs: where they'd push back on synchronous vs async; where humans must be in the loop; conservative-hold vs permissive-publish fraud policy by domain.
What they'd measure: index lag, DLQ depth, FP/FN per cohort, moderation backlog vs SLA, per-owner API error rate, webhook delivery success.

expectations: Names the source of truth and that search is a derived projection.
Knows POST should be 202 + async; can sketch a basic queue + worker pipeline.
Identifies that deduplication and fraud are 'a thing' but doesn't need full strategies.
Picks a real pattern for each pipeline stage (Outbox/CDC, blocking + ANN, rules + ML).
Discusses idempotency at both the HTTP and listing-content layer.
Explains read-path caching layers and what each absorbs.
Explicitly defends read/write isolation (stale-but-served) as the headline SLO.
Designs per-tenant partitioning + fair scheduling + hot-partition mitigation from the start.
Treats DLQ as a worklist with an operating loop; designs replay as a first-class workflow with versioned events.
Discusses fraud-model degradation policy, blue/green reindex rollback, version-conditional upsert, and a recovery story for each pipeline stage.
Backs saved/alerts/reports/webhooks with real tables and workers.
Designs paid promotion as a capped ranking term with relevance floor + slot-share cap + UI labeling; billing async on the read path; fraud always blocks paid amplification.
Connects FP/FN measurement, owner reputation features, and moderator labeling into a feedback loop.
Talks about cost: AI/geocoding budgets, search-cluster sizing per region, raw-file retention, media CDN egress.
Designs the system's failure modes by tenant cohort, not as a single platform.
Has an explicit story for safe rollouts of new enrichment models / fraud rules / index schema / event schema evolution.
Names RPO/RTO concretely; treats GDPR as a cross-cutting projection concern, not a bullet point.

Defines the metrics framework for the relevance-vs-revenue tradeoff (organic CTR, time-to-first-relevant-click, revenue per query) and gates boost-cap changes on A/B with seeker-experience guardrails.

followUps: How would your design change if FTP imports contained 100M rows (10x our peak)?
A partner sends the same logical listing through FTP yesterday and API today — walk through every layer that prevents a duplicate.
Rebuild the entire search index across all listings without downtime — how?
A bug marks 1M listings as duplicates. How do you recover, and how do you keep audit + provenance intact?
An old `listing.updated` event arrives at the indexer after a newer one. What happens, and where in the design is that protection?
How would you safely roll out a new fraud-scoring model without surprising trust-and-safety?
How would you let an owner see exactly why a specific listing was rejected, including the model version that scored it?
Multiple listing domains (jobs, real estate, vehicles) have very different schemas — how do you generalize without losing domain-specific fields?
Walk through GDPR erasure for one listing: which projections, caches, and stores does it touch?
Prevent search-result scraping without breaking legitimate seeker traffic.
Design the admin audit log so it's queryable, tamper-evident (hash-chained), and not the primary write bottleneck.
Make seeker search 50% cheaper — what's the first thing you'd cut?
A single owner is 50% of all platform writes. How do you keep the other 49,999 owners' SLOs intact?
An owner complains their boosted listing isn't showing up. How would you debug — and what could legitimately explain it (relevance floor, slot cap, exhausted budget, fraud hold)?
Design the metrics that tell you whether your promotion product is healthy or eroding seeker trust.
A high-spend owner asks for a guaranteed top slot. What do you offer instead?
How would you prevent click-fraud on promoted listings (an owner clicking their own ads to drain a competitor's budget)?
